We've got to be much more honest and go through this coarseness, and then we can get to a better state. But I do really feel that there is a lot of this still to be expressed. I don't know whether other people -

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes, in the right .... - not in the Puja.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: When we get together - that's why they're so chaotic sometimes.

turns.speaker: Ananda
paragraphs: This raises quite a serious difficulty in my mind. (General hilarity.)

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Just a minute, we're getting rather on with time.
(end of side one, side two)

We don't want to squash anything spontaneous, but at the same time we have to get through quite a lot of work, so make your point by all means, but I think we mustn't get into a sort of general discussion.

turns.speaker: Ananda
paragraphs: .................... if a coarse energy must be recognised, and I agree wholeheartedly with that, and then you say not in the Puja, that seems to me to create a split, and that certain energies are not going to be involved in the Puja, and that seems to be why a lot of Pujas are a little bit watery, and rather coarse.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: I mentioned a little while ago about people pumping their black, cold, negative energy into the Puja; it's better not to do the Puja at all, and to go away and work on that.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: That's not the same as coarseness.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: No; that is not the same as coarseness.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: Coarseness can be very positive. You can have coarse negative ......

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes, surely, yes.

turns.speaker: Ananda
paragraphs: But that's to be kept out of the Puja, you say?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: It's harsh; it's harsh, negative energy, it's to be kept out, yes. It isn't appropriate there at all.

turns.speaker: Ananda
paragraphs: I feel a bit of confusion of terms here, exactly what we mean by coarseness.

turns.speaker: Chintamani
paragraphs: Well, harsh, negative energy is simply unrecognised coarseness.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: No - no, I felt two things in these particular cases. I felt actually a lack of vitality; there is a lack of natural vitality. So things were having to be forced, because there was no flow. And also I felt - and I'm speaking now with reference to those whom I considered the worst offenders - I felt a lot of resentment, of a general nature, not specifically connected with the Puja, which had not been dealt with. So if you do the Puja with coarse energy, well that is a natural sort of energy; but in this case there was no energy, really, so therefore there had to be this forcing and pumping of energy, with great difficulty, into the Puja.

turns.speaker: Ananda
paragraphs: I'm a bit surprised, Bhante, that you say in that case don't do the Puja, because that seems to be the main vehicle of transforming one's energies.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: From coarse to fine, yes; but not if it's got so perverted and twisted as that. You need to work in a much more general way. If so many people are doing Puja and chanting in that way, it isn't a Puja; it's a sort of travesty of a Puja.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Right. You have to play entirely by ear, I think.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: I've felt this very strongly at some festivals, that it's been open to anybody to come along, ............. people who were committed ....... to be involved ....

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Let's just come on to this, then, for a minute, because we're going to stop for coffee in ten minutes' time - this question of festivals, since it has come up, and I have one or two thoughts

about this. It has seemed to me more and more that festivals in this country, which is a non- Buddhist country, can't be public, in the way that they are in the East. In an Eastern Buddhist country that's got a nominally Buddhist population, which has got some devotion for Buddhism but which doesn't really concern itself with Buddhism very much most of the time, but they feel very happy to go along once or twice a year to a really big do, a big festival, and bring flowers and light candles, and they do feel genuine devotion. So you've got hundreds and thousands of them flocking in, and you can have a very positive atmosphere. But that is not the case in this country, so I think we ought to consider whether festivals should not be confined to the Order and Mitras and perhaps a few invited Friends; and not expect the general public to come, have something else for the general public if you want to involve them, if you want to involve the general public in your neighbourhood, have something entirely different on some other occasion which will be much more suitable.

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: What we found at Archway recently is that - well, we just haven't had room to open it to the public, in a way. We started with an Order celebration, then the Mitras have come in, then in the evening there's a general celebration for Friends who regularly attend the Centre. But a lot of other people do - I don't know where they come from, how they hear about it - they come, but they seen to be people who really want to come to a festival and get a great deal from it. And that seems to be quite a good arrangement.

turns.speaker: Manjuvajra
paragraphs: I remember that occasion when you gave your talk. Over half had never been before.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: What day was that?

turns.speaker: Manjuvajra
paragraphs: Dharmachakra.

turns.speaker: Padmaraja
paragraphs: They're not publicly advertised?

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: They're not advertised at all.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well, if people come along spontaneously, scenting a festival in the air, fair enough.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: I don't know - my mind keeps going back to those dreadful Wesaks at Caxton Hall, under the auspices of the Buddhist Society. They were well advertised, and people came along if they were connected with the society - but they were really awful, not a spark of joy.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: I think all those I've been to recently have been really good. A lot of people happen to hear about it, and they do come along - really ........... very, very positive, and that kind of positivity is what I was talking about when I was answering Manjuvajra - there are occasions when people - it can be sensitive, it can be something in the air that's really good, and it does ......... on festival days, sometimes it's really good if the public can just come.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: This started happening ............ advertising.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: And also it's been presumably, a neighbourhood thing.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: No.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: No?

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: Well, in that there are certain communities round there, yes.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: ............... we had an open day, and two people outside the Friends, from the neighbourhood, came. (Voices.)

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Good. But mainly with Order members and Mitras and Friends.

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: That's the basis. And it builds up to maybe half the people we've never seen before. But they were really getting into it.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: And it's much better now we've restored the .............. instead of just ..........

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well, perhaps if you have a festival for yourselves, you'll start enjoying it, and because you're enjoying it other people will be attracted; but if you're laying on a festival for the public, perhaps not the same thing happens. If you have a good time yourselves, others may be attracted, because they feel that you're having a good time.

turns.speaker: Manjuvajra
paragraphs: That seemed to be very prominent on Padmasambhava Day, where it's recognised that a lot of people had a connection with Padmasambhava, .... spiritual life in general, These things do draw a lot of Order members in, and in the evening there were a lot more people than usually came to that class that evening. There were quite a lot of people, it wasn't advertised at all. It wasn't even advertised, I believe, actually inside the Friends, to the people who normally come along. But a lot more people did come along.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: So the moral seems pretty obvious, doesn't it? If you're positive yourself you'll draw in other people.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: My first ...... contact with the Friends was through a festival, through Wesak.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: The one at Napier Hall? Yes, I remember that. But there were criticisms afterwards from within the Friends that the chanting went on too long, and so on and so forth.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: Oh, .... it was about an hour and a half long, walking and chanting, I seem to remember. And then a Puja, .... really into it.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: No time for my talk, even! (Laughter, voices.)

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Anything more about festivals? - and then we can come back to ceremonies again after the coffee break.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: Which ones do we celebrate? .......

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well, there is a list in Shabda which Devaraja prepared. I have all the Shabdas here.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: That includes Padmapani's release, and -

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Does anyone remember which ...... (Voices.) - which Shabda it was in?

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: February or March ...... last year.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Last year?

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: January of this year. (Voices.)

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes, dead right. The calendar of festivals for 1975: Wednesday, February 26, the Buddha's Mahaparinirvana. Then April 6, Eighth Anniversary of the Founding of the FWBO. April 7, anniversary of the Founding of the WBO. April 8, Buddha's Birthday. May 25,

Wesak, Bodhi Day. July 23, Dharmachakra Day. August 12, Padmasambhava Day. October 20, Sangha Day. And then January 7, Bodhidharma Day. And then there were additional days of importance: May 20, Tara's birth. July 13, Manjusri's Liberation. November 24, Avalokitesvara's Liberation. October 17, Vajrapani's Liberation. December 24, Bodhidharma arrives in China! (Laughter.)

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: ........... celebrating or recognition of something that I felt to be of great importance - and other people have expressed this as well - and that is the moment of leaving home, and in terms of the way, our approach to -

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Whose moment of leaving home? The Buddha's, yes.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: .......... the moment of the Buddha - Siddhartha Gautama - leaving home. It had been suggested that this should be - that it would be a good idea to celebrate that moment.

turns.speaker: Nagabodhi
paragraphs: That's a very good idea.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: It is on a full-moon day, I don't remember which one it is. It may overlap with another, later full-moon event, but we can certainly quite easily find out which full moon it was. Lokamitra: Can we just go through these a bit? At the last Convention I think we agreed to celebrate the FWBO, the WBO Day, the Buddha's birthday, Wesak, Dharmachakra Day, and Sangha Day publicly; and then perhaps a Chinese celebration, which Bodhidharma Day was chosen, I think, after consultation with Mr. Chen. But -

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: As regards the date, you mean?

turns.speaker: Lokamitra
paragraphs: Yes. I'm not quite sure, exactly. But I don't think we agreed to celebrate the Parinirvana Day, but it seems to me it's a very good thing that we do, although perhaps not as a major festival.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: What has occurred to me is that on the Parinirvana Day we could well - though this question hasn't quite arisen yet - we could well perhaps remember Order members and Friends who are no longer with us in this world. It might be good to combine it with that.

turns.speaker: Lokamitra
paragraphs: The point I was going to make was that I wonder - I think we ought to cut or we oughtn't to enlarge too much on our main festivals, like day-long festivals, because otherwise we'll have too many. And at the moment some aren't being celebrated that well, for example WBO Day was celebrated very poorly.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: Where?

turns.speaker: Lokamitra
paragraphs: In Archway. Sorry, I should be more specific. I think there were about seven - eight of us the whole day. That was the WBO Day. So if we choose to celebrate something, we should really celebrate it, and we should decide which ones we're going to really celebrate, so that everyone is clear in their own minds -

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: And they don't clash with retreats. (Voices.)

turns.speaker: Lokamitra
paragraphs: And then others - perhaps Padmasambhava Day would be a specifically Order Day, usually, and Parinirvana perhaps we won't celebrate it for the whole day.

turns.speaker: Aryamitra
paragraphs: The important one are the Buddha, Dharma and Sangha Days, Wesak, and the Friends and WBO Days.

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: WBO Day is ..... Order.

turns.speaker: Vajradaka
paragraphs: As far as the FWBO Day goes, ....... there was an intimation, a movement up in Scotland, that celebration of FWBO was FWBO London, and that they wanted to celebrate FWBO Glasgow. The FWBO was founded in Glasgow.

turns.speaker: Lokamitra
paragraphs: It wasn't the foundation of FWBO London, though, it was the foundation of FWBO, full stop.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: It happened to be in London.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: I must add, though, that the same feeling was expressed in New Zealand.

turns.speaker: Sanghamitta
paragraphs: I think it's necessary to .............. of celebrations ........

turns.speaker: Aryamitra
paragraphs: Well, that would be, if you're connected with a Centre, ......... activities ..........

turns.speaker: Sanghamitta
paragraphs: Yes, but somebody might want to come up to Archway ............ ........ special arrangements ........... (Voices.)

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: What festivals have we decided?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well, let's take Wesak first, I think that's agreed upon, isn't it? May 25th.

turns.speaker: Lokamitra
paragraphs: Do we call it Bodhi Day or Wesak?

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: I suggest we call it .... Wesak.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well, what about Buddha Day? It's often called Buddha Day in India now.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: Vaishakha's the second best ... (Voices.) Better than Wesak.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: If you think it necessary, you can put Vaishakha in in brackets, Vaishakha Purnima. Vaishakha is the Sanskrit word for the name of that month. Vesakha is the Pali form, and Wesak is the Sinhalese form. It's the full moon day of that Vaishakha, Vissakha or Wesak month.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: I think Buddha Day would be an English version, it's more .....

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: I think it's generally agreed we celebrate, and really celebrate, that. All right, let's pass on, then, rather quickly. Dharma Day, which is Dharmachakra Day.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: Call it Dharma Day.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well, it is really Dharma Day.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: I like Dharmachakra. (Voices.)

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well, we are celebrating the Dharma, and especially the Buddha's first teaching of the Dharma, but there are doubts, as you know, about the Dharmachakrapravartana Sutra. We don't really know what the Buddha did say. We only know that he said something, and that something was the Dharma; and that everything else that he said subsequently was the Dharma.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes, in a very broad sense, yes; it's the teaching that he gave out, whatever that might have been.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: It's nice to have three days, one called Buddha Day, one called Dharma Day and one called Sangha Day.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes, that is the main consideration. (Voices considering.)

turns.speaker: Mangala
paragraphs: I think it .... easier ..... a little bit confused as to what Dharmachakra Day is, you might think .....

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well, the idea actually is that in the course of the year we have - our three main festivals are dedicated to the Three Jewels. I think this is the main thing to be stressed.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: What about celebrating our ordination - ?

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: Should we finish this - ?

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: Oh, I thought we had finished.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: No. And then Sangha Day, which is October 20th. I may not - I'll have to look into that, I rather suspect it should be in November. I think it's best to have it four months - does that make any difference, from other practical points of view, whether we have it in October or November? I think it would be more correct to have it in November - four months -

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: Does Dharmachakra follow close on the heels of Buddha Day?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well, it has to, because it's two months after, two full-moon days after. Whereas I think it would be better if the Sangha Day was at the end of the four-month rainy season retreat rather than the three-month one, the four month one being the full one.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: Why is that, Bhante?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well, you could either stay three months in your rains retreat or four, but four was considered, in a way, better. Then the giving of the new robes took place at the end of four months, which brings me to ....., which is November, roughly. That's how it's usually celebrated in India.

turns.speaker: Lokamitra
paragraphs: We usually take the full moon day of October-November, so sometimes we do have it in November.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Hm. Well, in that case it will be all right. But we'll have to look into it a bit more closely.

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: So what are we actually saying? - that it should be four months from -

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well, it should be four months -

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: Four moon months.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Four lunar months from Dharmachakra Day.

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: And would that apply to this year? Because we have already agreed -

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: No, since we've already agreed, let it be as it is.

turns.speaker: Asvajit
paragraphs: How was Padmasambhava Day decided, Bhante?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: I think mainly because we felt him to be a generally appealing spiritual figure, and also because the majority of my own teachers belonged to that particular lineage.

turns.speaker: Asvajit
paragraphs: Oh yes, I'm aware of all that; I mean that particular date.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes, that is the traditional Tibetan ....... day for Padmasambhava, fifth day of fifth month (?).

So we've got this group, first of all, of three: Buddha Day, Dharma Day, Sangha Day, which are the three great celebrations of our year, public celebrations. Then Padmasambhava Day is also a public celebration, isn't it?

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: No, it's been just the Order.

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: It's been somewhere between the two. Order most of the day and then in the evening it's open. Quite quiet, for some reason.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Fair enough. And then what about the Buddha's birthday and the Parinirvana Day, which are according to the Far Eastern calendar?

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: I think they should be a bit the same as Padmasambhava Day, during the day it should be Order and Mitras and quite quiet. On Parinirvana Day this year we played a tape, and then had a slightly more elaborate Puja. It was quite successful.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Good. And, of course, leading up the Buddha's birthday, we've got the anniversary of the FWBO and the anniversary of the WBO.

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: What we have been doing so far for the FWBO celebrations - well, this year ...........

turns.speaker: Lokamitra
paragraphs: It took place on Mitra Day.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: I arrived in London that weekend, and I was there for the Order anniversary, and apparently arrangements had rather fallen through, and Lokamitra and I just hastily improvised something; and that was rather a pity, that no thought or preparation apparently had been made for the celebration of the Order anniversary.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes, but that wasn't enough. That was good enough for the retreat.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: Perhaps we ought to make that a major festival.

turns.speaker: Lokamitra
paragraphs: Well, it is a major festival. (Voices ruminating.)

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: 6th April is the anniversary of the founding of the FWBO, the eighth anniversary this year, and April 7th the seventh anniversary of the WBO.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: I think next year there has to be more discussion. It also has occurred to me that we could have those three days next year as an Order Convention, or include that time; my idea being that we should have the Order Convention around our anniversary, and next year just have three or four days, and then the year after that again have a big one like this.

turns.speaker: Ananda
paragraphs: It would make it rather soon after this one, though, wouldn't it, barely six months?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Oh yes, that's why we're going to have a shorter one. Because this one is late; it's six months late ...

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: I'm also thinking in terms of - just as we had (time is getting on, maybe we ought to stop soon and carry on again) - but what I was thinking - that I'd like to develop a sort of pattern whereby there's a sort of concentration of study retreats, August and September, even October, which would mean that we couldn't meet at this time for an Order Convention, and have the Order Convention in the spring, say Convention in the spring and then six months later study retreats in the autumn, which means half way through the year we'd be getting together in one way or another, at least some of us. This is my rough idea.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: Sounds as though, season-wise, ...... study ...

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Anyway, perhaps we'll stop now and we can think about this during the coffee break and then express our ideas afterwards.
(Break)

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: We'd got a little bit off festivals but on to something quite important, which was the suggestion I put forward that we have our next Convention, a short one, in the early spring, about Easter, and then keep the autumn hopefully for study seminars. So could we hear how people feel about that to begin with, as briefly as possible, as a suggestion?

turns.speaker: Manjuvajra
paragraphs: I've got one rather selfish point, and that is that because of the nature of the work I'm doing I have to have my holidays at certain times in the year, and I think there are quite a few Order members who are doing the same work, and if that could be borne in mind it would be greatly appreciated.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: .......... Easter holidays?

turns.speaker: Manjuvajra
paragraphs: Yes, we have Easter holidays, but quite often things occur just after, for example, the Order retreat after this which I would have like to have stayed to, but it goes into the beginning of term.

turns.speaker: Lokamitra
paragraphs: Just one point that comes to mind is that it does ....... if necessary and there may well be many more members or three or four members of the Order next year. If we can't use Tittleshall it'll be much, much more expensive and most of us, devoting more and more time to the work we do, can't afford very much; so cost is important to consider .....

turns.speaker: Manjuvajra
paragraphs: There's one way that you could get round that, and this is what I was thinking of when I was thinking about organising the Convention, and that was to put up a tent, like it would be very easy ..........

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: You can't do it at Easter very easily, not necessarily .....

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: ... three-day Convention.

turns.speaker: Lokamitra
paragraphs: It's not that. You can't rely on the weather at Easter, mate.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: You can't rely on the weather at any time.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: ......... three days -

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: No, I think we have not to get involved in discussion about the practical arrangements. I think those will have to be left to the Order Convenor. I think at the moment let's just think in terms of whether we do like that idea of the sort of distribution of the Order year, as it were, with the Convention - with next year a short one at Easter and if possible during the school holiday - with study seminars organised, as and when we do have them, in the course of the autumn.

turns.speaker: Asvajit
paragraphs: Seems a natural sort of order.

turns.speaker: Ananda
paragraphs: Good in principle, but I do feel it's important to bear in mind that the Convention achieves its purpose if as many people as possible are able to come on it. I think that must be the priority - the time must be chosen when most people can come.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well, possibly no time of year, then, would be better than Easter.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: Should it be definitely Easter rather than ........

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Doesn't that come during that period?

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: No, it doesn't. (Voices.)

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: When is Easter next year?

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: Easter is April 18th. (Voices.)

turns.speaker: Ananda
paragraphs: I don't see any overriding need for it to coincide with the Buddha Day anniversary.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: No, I wasn't suggesting Buddha Day, but the Order anniversary.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: I think that would be quite difficult, but maybe ....... and may be impossible to get ......

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Right, yes. Perhaps we can leave it simply at this, that we have it at Easter or early in the spring, and we can only decide exactly when when the accommodation position has been investigated; and no doubt Khema as Order Convenor or Convention Convenor will then put up a proposal.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: Wouldn't that be an advantage of having it in the summer .... different time for the retreat and the Convention? Lokamitra: It's also a very important period, really, for retreats to happen, at the end of that term; so you'd have to have it at one end of the Easter school holidays.

turns.speaker: Manjuvajra
paragraphs: I think it's very good to have it in spring, especially if it's after the winter, when you've spent a period of incubation (?) it's nice to come together .........

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Hopefully; I'm not making any definite promise. I'm just thinking in this way that that would be a good way of distributing Order activities.
: .... good weather, it isn't too hot .........

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: All right, I think details will have to be worked out at a later stage, and people will be informed accordingly, bearing in mind all these various points that have been mentioned, and trying to ensure as many people as possible being able to gather together for the Convention.

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: I'd like to go back to a couple of points on the Puja. They are interrelated. The first one is that .... at least an issue when we were first investigating ...., trying to be quite careful about what image we were giving and the impression people were getting of the way in which .......... ourselves and so forth. We thought that maybe it wouldn't be appropriate to use our Order names and perhaps even consider not using kesas. I have actually since changed my mind about this, but I want to raise it because several people were involved in the earlier discussion about it. Perhaps just a general principle is it's not always going to be appropriate to use those things. People do react ....

turns.speaker: Ananda
paragraphs: You mean in a specifically teaching situation?

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: Well, just in general. We thought what the problem .... people would be very sensitive about anything foreign, you know, from outside, and not English.

turns.speaker: Chintamani
paragraphs: Yes. I'd be very - .... changing mind, but I'd be very cautious about anything like this, about dropping the names and dropping the use of the kesa. I think we should be thinking much more in the other direction, using the kesas more often and insisting that we use our names more often.

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: What I've since felt is that people will respond more to that than to ...... It's a tremendous opening (?) - people asking why we changed our names and so on, why we wear kesas. It's a good excuse to ....

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: What about Order members in Norfolk? Have they had any experience in this connection?

turns.speaker: Devamitra
paragraphs: Well, I teach my own class under my Christian name. I've never even thought of using my Order name................ any events other than ....... Pujas and the festivals.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Didn't you say something once about the postman knowing that - ?

turns.speaker: Devamitra
paragraphs: Oh, yes!

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: And local people knowing that you had Buddhist names and that you had made that clear?

turns.speaker: Devamitra
paragraphs: We've certainly made that clear and we gave the Post Office our Christian names and our Buddhist names.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: What reaction did you encounter?

turns.speaker: Devamitra
paragraphs: Interest. Certainly not a negative reaction.

turns.speaker: Ratnapani
paragraphs: I just can't imagine not using my ordained name now, I just don't answer to the other one. If the neighbours hear Devamitra yelling across the garden, 'Ratnapani, the tea's ready', I don't think they bat an eyelid. I think they just accept that that's us. ...........

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: What I feel is that the greatest importance psychologically, especially with, for want of a better term, ordinary people, is that you yourself are full of confidence. That if you say, 'That's my name,' they don't bat an eyelid, that's your name. But if you say, 'I do have - er - a Buddhist name (laughter) - actually, though, if you like you can call me - George', they won't know where they are, but if you say, 'My name's Ratnapani' or 'My name's Devamitra' or 'My name's Subhuti', that's that. It's your positiveness which communicates far more.

turns.speaker: Ratnapani
paragraphs: And that is your name, it's not an added thing which you've got, it is your name.

turns.speaker: Ananda
paragraphs: .........., I feel that I've personally encountered not negativity but awe - the fact that this name has very definite Eastern connotations. People think, we're Indian with Indian connections or an Indian background

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well, so we do, in a way.

turns.speaker: Ananda
paragraphs: Yes, but - and then I feel that I've got to explain, 'We're Westerners, but we're simply bringing Buddhism to the West.'

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well, that's good, because you've got a chance then to talk about the whole Movement.

turns.speaker: Ananda
paragraphs: You don't think it's an impediment that people have this association with India and the East?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well, in a sense it is, but it's absolutely unavoidable, at present. Because if you mention Buddhism - well, it's a teaching of Indian origin in a sense. But I think if they're in contact with you and they can see that you're English - as much English as they are, if not more so - they're not going to bother about the name sounding a bit oriental. There's lots of odd names around, especially in London, all sorts of names. You don't know where they come from, sometimes. They're much more odd than Ananda or Subhuti; some of them much more difficult to pronounce.

turns.speaker: Sanghamitta
paragraphs: [I was] in contact recently, though, with somebody I was training. She thought that it was Indian, and ........ Indian ............. ............. !

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well, you can't help all that sort of thing, you just have to deal with it -

turns.speaker: Padmaraja
paragraphs: Subhuti, you say you recently found it more fruitful to use your Buddhist name down in Bethnal Green?

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: I think people there are quite responsive to something structured and formal and definite.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: One thing that occurs to me is that - don't forget there you're dealing with, inverted commas, 'working-class' people, and their reactions are quite different from those of middle- class people, and it's mainly middle-class people that we've encountered so far. For instance, what about the approach of quite ordinary working-class boys and men to karate, and the strictness, the discipline, the names, the Japanese terms, the bowing? They take it absolutely in their stride. They like it. It's definite, it's positive.

turns.speaker: Lokamitra
paragraphs: But you haven't used your ordained name, have you?

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: To some extent, yes, with one or two people.

turns.speaker: Lokamitra
paragraphs: In what sort of situations?

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: I use it for karate...... . And I shall certainly use it more; well, I shall use it completely.

turns.speaker: Vajradaka
paragraphs: So do you use it with ..... at Bethnal Green?

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: No.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: We had to make our initial approach - and we were very cautious about that - before they even met us, when we were corresponding.

turns.speaker: Ratnapani
paragraphs: It's been the same in my contact with officials in Norfolk, it's just been obvious that I use my ordinary name.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: I wondered whether, when we were sending out these letters for the - what is it? these fund-raising letters, if the foreign- sounding names will have a detrimental effect on the fund-raising scene.

turns.speaker: Lokamitra
paragraphs: I think there we play it by ear, so that some of us use our ordained name, some of us use our main (?maiden!) name.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Anyway, one more point connected with use of names, before we get back to the beaten track: the use of Order names within the Order. I think there has been a bit of criticism recently that not all Order members use their Order names within the Order. (Voices in concern.) Yes, this is what I have been told - that Order members talking to one another sometimes are still heard to use the old names. I've overheard this once or twice myself, I must say - Order members addressing one another by their old names. (Murmurs.) Well, your guess is as good as mine.

turns.speaker: Lokamitra
paragraphs: I don't think it does now. It used to, and then it became just a slip of the tongue, and I don't, personally I don't find it now.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: But has anyone noticed this happening at all? (Voices.)

turns.speaker: Sanghamitta
paragraphs: ....... myself ........... because I always ........ ......... why I did it.

turns.speaker: Buddhadasa
paragraphs: A little bit more insidious is - it's OK for a slip of the tongue - but the shortening of names. For example, one is, I know some people call you 'Dad' .........; the other is Vajrakumara sometimes people call Vajra. (Laughter.)

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: I think more often than not, in my experience, that's like an affectionate -

turns.speaker: Buddhadasa
paragraphs: Well, you can call me Buddha any time you like! (Remarks; laughter.)

turns.speaker: Jitari
paragraphs: You call me 'jittery' and you see what happens. [Laughter]

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: ............ something that ..... has a relationship to the original word - name.

turns.speaker: Ratnapani
paragraphs: I put this to Bhante some months ago, Devamitra and I spoke to him and he said (if one may use your words) more or less that it's OK if the abbreviation makes up a complete word, like 'Vajra' is a word - 'Vajji' actually is a bit different. .... a complete term -

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: I do think at the same time that the complete name is better, but if the abbreviation is a complete word it isn't so objectionable.

turns.speaker: Sanghamitta
paragraphs: And if you don't mind, I'd like to hear Bhante called Bhante and not 'Bunty'!

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: If you want to be very correct, it's (pronouncing very distinctly) B H A N T E . Almost everybody misses that aspirate.

turns.speaker: Sanghamitta
paragraphs: I think you used to get 'Bunty', ....... comic .....

turns.speaker: Bodhisri
paragraphs: ..................... so you have to stop that ....... and ..... But if you could sort of abbreviate or make them indistinguishable .......... it would be all right.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: You could possibly do what the Tibetans have done and translate the name. For instance, the Tibetans wouldn't call someone Vajra, they'd call him Dorje - dorje is the Tibetan translation for Vajra.

turns.speaker: Bodhisri
paragraphs: There is a Finnish word for - very similar to vajra ...... that means hammer. It's ....

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: I don't think we'd better call Vajrabodhi that. Somebody else might be the nail! [Laughter]

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: It goes very deep, though, the hammer of Thor ...........

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: I've even been wondering about Celtic names. But I've been looking through Celtic myth and legend, but names of most Celtic heroes and heroines have got most unfortunate associations; they do all sorts of horrific things in these legends, so the associations aren't always very positive.

turns.speaker: Ananda
paragraphs: In the future, Bhante, do you think the Indian associations of the Order will continue to be significant in terms of names and things like that?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: I'm not sure what you mean by significant.

turns.speaker: Ananda
paragraphs: Well, do you think we'll continue to - that you, as a specific, will continue to give people Indian names?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well, I've only got another 20 or 30 years to go. It'll be decided maybe hundreds of years after that; I don't know. It will be up to you or the generation after you.

turns.speaker: Ananda
paragraphs: Have you any particular feeling about what the trend should be?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: I think it's much more likely that Indian names will become naturalised. What about the so-called Christian names? Nobody feels George and John as foreign any more, but they're not British. What is British, anyway?
Anyway, a few words perhaps on the question of kesas. Has anyone observed any irregularity in the matter of kesas?

turns.speaker: Sanghamitta
paragraphs: They're still dirty sometimes.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: I think the main point that I made originally was that kesas should always be worn for Pujas, and on those occasions where Order members need to be identified by members of the public, and if people feel like wearing their kesas for their own personal meditation and Puja that's up to them. And also when they as it were represent the Order, when they hold the
Convention, when they (noise) ..................
(end of tape 5)
Tape 6, side 1
if they are giving public ..... and are speaking as a member of the Order, then they should wear their kesa.

turns.speaker: Sanghamitta
paragraphs: I think it's very important, if they are wearing it as members of the Order ....., that they should be clean, because if somebody asks you what it is, .........it's not very ........ (laughter)

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: I think that should apply whether you're outside the Movement or within it.

turns.speaker: Sanghamitta
paragraphs: Yes, especially outside, because we can tell one another off inside, it's rather difficult outside, isn't it? It gives a sort of hippy impression or something.

turns.speaker: Asvajit
paragraphs: ......... if you see a Order member wearing a really filthy kesa and lots of other -

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: I think the point is taken that the kesa should be clean. And if your kesa is grubby and can't be washed, then replace it.

turns.speaker: Dharmapala
paragraphs: The kesa itself has been drawn from an Eastern tradition as far as I know, and I presume everything about it has a significance. What I often see is the cord that's on the bottom of it - this is very often plaited and people tie little knots in it, things like that, do you feel that that is ..... ?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: No, I think it should be uniform. Gotami made a standard pattern originally, and as far as I know that is the one that we are following, or should be following. That's the one I took to Zena(?)'s? ...... copy .....

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: ........ embroidery, it's ........ gold thread or something like that, is that ..... ?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: We experimented with that gold, metallic thread in the early days. It was found very difficult to manipulate, so therefore we got the nearest that we could in silk thread, and you can't always get exactly the same shade, unfortunately. But this is why the colour of that yellow silk does vary a bit from kesa to kesa sometimes.

turns.speaker: Sanghamitta
paragraphs: I think the knots in the cord ......... somewhere right down here, ........... two knots in it -

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: I was given one in New Zealand, one .... I took up, that the cord hung right down to somebody's knees! [Laughter] - just that one, so I got somebody in Auckland to shorten it. I don't know who made that one.
Sanghamitta No, well, mine's like that, I had to put two knots in -

turns.speaker: Sanghamitta
paragraphs: Oughtn't we to make it a standard size?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well, yes, Gotami has worked out or given the standard specifications, and those are the ones which should be followed. Are copies of those specifications in circulation? Well, we
should just follow those carefully.

turns.speaker: Asvajit
paragraphs: You can also get exactly the same colour, even in New Zealand. (Voices.)

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: Maybe the whole ..... should be published in Shabda again so you can circulate .....

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: That's a good idea, yes.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: So there's no question about it.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: .....instituted a kind of practice, almost -

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Right, yes.

turns.speaker: Ananda
paragraphs: Well, why not issue complete instructions for making the kesa in Shabda?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: I think that's a good idea. (Voices.) Well, that pattern must be communicated to them, and they shouldn't be allowed to make kesas without the proper pattern.

turns.speaker: Marichi
paragraphs: People put knots in them because they're different heights. Tall people don't need to put as many knots in them as short people.

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: No, I think that's because kesas have increased in width, for instance, by about an inch since - my kesa's very narrow. They're gradually getting wider and wider.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well, let the original standard specifications be printed in Shabda, and let's make sure that we stick to those. Anyway, let's not spend any more time on that. We have to get back to festivals and to Bodhidharma Day. What do we say about that?

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: I've still got one more point arising out of this, which is the Puja itself. It's often seemed to me that without quite a bit of explanation or quite a bit of uncritical openness on the part of those partaking in the Puja, it's not very explicit. There are things in it which aren't very clear, and even quite misleading, and for all that we say -

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: You mean the words of the Puja?

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: The words of the Puja. For all that we say we're not worshipping an external deity and there's no evil in Buddhism and so forth, this can come across if you don't understand the Puja. I think it time that we really thought about having a much more explicit simplified version of the Puja, and I am thinking in particular ..... Bethnal Green.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: As a matter of fact, we did have a discussion about this in Helsinki, didn't we? And some of the Friends who were attending there, one in particular, felt that the Sevenfold Puja should not just be available to the public: from his point of view, more particularly because certain sentiments were expressed which could not genuinely be the sentiments of someone who was not really devoted or even committed to Buddhism. So I thought about it, and I said I thought that very likely there should be a sort of very simple Puja for more general and public use, which didn't contain any problematical material, just express simple devotion.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: Would you include the Heart Sutra?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: I think so, yes; I mean I think that I'd include it in those reservations - that it is in a sense nonsense to the average person who just comes along for the first time, or even the tenth time.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: What about Going for Refuge?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: This again is the path of regular steps that I've been thinking about quite a lot. I have been - there's much to be said about this, from several points of view, but I have been thinking we must apply the path of regular steps more strictly, and perhaps have, for more general use when there'll be newcomers around, a Puja which simply expresses respect for the Buddha as teacher explicitly, and then ............ (aircraft noise) .........................

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well, I'm thinking especially of a new Puja for more general public use, keeping the Sevenfold Puja within the Order, mainly, and having -

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: .................... the things that you suggest ....... ?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: For instance, my idea is - these are just tentative, but my idea is that when people assemble those who wish should have the opportunity of offering a stick of incense, lighting a candle, and maybe giving a flower. And then there should be simple verses recited by the leader - first of all, expressing general devotion to or respect for the Buddha as Enlightened human teacher, and then saying that we offer these flowers which remind us of the impermanence of all mundane things, and therefore remind us to fix our minds on higher things - something of this nature. And then we offer the light which symbolises the Light of Truth which we aspire to kindle in our own hearts; and then that we light this stick of incense or offer this stick of incense, which represents the fragrance of the positive life expanding in all directions. And then after that there can be a suitable reading, perhaps even a quite lengthy one, and then the chanting, to a musical tune, of, say, the Avalokitesvara mantra. I think for general use something like this.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes, I think I'd have to think about it myself and talk to a few other people. There are traditional Pali verses which could be adapted, which express exactly these sort of things. So what do you think of this? (Murmurs of approval.)

turns.speaker: Sanghamitta
paragraphs: I think it would make it more meaningful to the public. I'm sure a lot of it they don't understand, and -

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: And it would, of course, all be in English.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: It would lead on to -

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: But if anybody feels like writing some verses of this kind, by all means do so, and just send them along to me and then we'll all discuss them.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: I think Gotami's been producing some .....................

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well, if she has I'm sure she'll let us know in due course.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: But I don't think ............. would be suitable ........

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: That's different, that's a place for the Sevenfold Puja.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: Could you just ......... which Pali verses....... did you have in mind?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Let me just think. Puja .....(chants briefly in Pali) - verses like that, I'm not going to recite them all but I can tell you where to find them. There is a little book called The Mirror of the Dharma, which is published in Ceylon. I think you can get copies from the Vihara. We've got copies, I think, at Aryatara, or did have. The Mirror of the Dharma ..... They can be very freely adapted, and the words must be suitable and poetic in .... way.

turns.speaker: Vajradaka
paragraphs: Does The Mirror of the Dharma contain verses in English?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: The verses are all in Pali, with an English translation. But the English translation will need adaptation. Even if one produces verses of one's own of the same kind, that's fine. These particular verses aren't sacrosanct. They were composed by monks at different times.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: Do you think lines like 'The evil which I have heaped up' - do you think it would be more appropriate to have 'The faults which I have heaped up', bearing in mind that the title of that particular section is the Confession of Faults?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: I think 'the evil which you have heaped up' is unskilfulness of body, speech and mind which is now catching up with you.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: The actual term 'evil' .................. ........ the actual word 'evil'.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: It's a bit like - I was wondering if it was a bit like 'the mistakes I've ....'

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: The difficulty with English is either the word is too strong or it's too weak. 'Faults' is a bit weak, actually, really; 'Confession of Faults' - you're really confessing much more than your faults. If you only had faults, you'd be a pretty advanced sort of person, but it's much worse than that, really.

turns.speaker: Ratnapani
paragraphs: The new Puja would take care of that, wouldn't it, if there is a new Puja, because we understand ........'evil'?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes, we understand, yes, right.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: I'm sure we do. By long acquaintance.

turns.speaker: Manjuvajra
paragraphs: Yes, indeed. It's a good opportunity to use the Puja as a sort of basis for a study group. I've done that in Truro and found it really useful, and maybe even just one section a week.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Is that all about Puja?

turns.speaker: Uttara
paragraphs: I think there should be a morning - a morning Puja, not the Sevenfold Puja but an actual something different.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: There's the Tiratana Vandana .... English version.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: We finish morning meditations at the Centre with the Puja,and that's -

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: I think there is something to be said for a morning Puja in the sense of a Puja which is recited only in the morning, and utilises the symbolical associations of morning and the rising of the sun and the spreading of light. There are some verses of this nature; I'll try to look them up and see if I can produce anything.

turns.speaker: Dharmapala
paragraphs: Did you say it neutralises the symbolical associations?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Utilises. (Voices: Utilises. Utilises.) Makes use of.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: Are we talking about ceremonies at the moment?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: At the moment, we are, though we've got to come back to festivals quite quickly.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: There's something that has not yet as far as I'm aware happened in the Order yet, but there seems to be in process to some of those whom we know, and that is a death. I was wondering whether or not we had any - I know I don't have any real clear idea of the procedure that we have to set up for that. I was thinking maybe some -

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: I have explained this and I'm going to explain it again to Jinamata, because there has been a death, not within the Order but certainly of a Friend, someone known to quite a few people, it seems, and there is going to be, I believe, a ceremony for that person. And Jinamata, I believe - I'm not completely certain - is going to conduct it ..... I understand she wants to get together with me about it. I did give a few notes to Lokamitra, but she wants even further clarification. There is a standard procedure. I think I just can't go into it now, because there isn't simply time, but perhaps - what we really need, I think, is - this question has been raised before - an Order Puja book, containing all ceremonies that an Order member may be called upon to perform, the text of them, with directions.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: I was wondering whether or not - what the law is regarding a dead person, whether there could be any kind of ceremony with the deceased there.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: The law is, as far as I know, that the last word as regards the kind of ceremonies rests with the next of kin - unfortunately, but this is the law. If the next of kin want you, even though you're an Order member, or have been, to be buried with full Church of England rites, there's nothing that the Order can do about it. But there's nothing to prevent the Order after, say, seven days, or whatever the period might be, from having its own memorial service; but the disposal of the body will be in accordance with the wishes of the next of kin, so even if you leave written instructions, these can be disregarded.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: Could an agreement be reached with one's next of kin? I mean, for example, can one say who one's next of kin is?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: As far as I know in law it has to be either husband or wife or close blood relations like mother, father, brother, sister.

turns.speaker: Sanghamitta
paragraphs: Or they have to state in their will something that they want, don't they?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: That can be set aside by the next of kin. The only way you can get round it is by making it conditional, say, in this way, that you leave them such-and-such sum of money but on condition that they dispose of you in such-and-such a way. But if you haven't got anything to leave, well.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: I've already asked my brother if he would ask a real Buddhist ..........

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: But whatever request one may leave, it can be set aside, because there was an instance of this in the case of a friend of mine, who left written instructions that he should have a Buddhist funeral and that I should conduct it, and that was set aside by his parents, who gave him a Church of England funeral and buried him.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: It seems that what may happen is that in one of our Buddhist communities, one of the Order communities, even, someone may die, maybe even in the community, and very quickly ..... rushes off to the morgue, with all sorts of things go on there, I've heard, that are just quite horrible - you know, there's no reverence shown to the deceased, it's really treating them in an awful way, just like so much rubbish.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well, you can delay your notification - I'm not quite sure what the law is, but I think you have to notify the police, in the case of a death quite -

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: Perhaps these are things that the communities themselves, or certain Centres themselves, certain communities, ...... important point because it's something that can really be a very enriching thing -

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: But I think from the legal point of view we'd have to ask someone to look into this.

turns.speaker: Sanghamitta
paragraphs: Can I just clarify something? I felt very strongly about when Ali died ..............., I'd just like to have five minutes to explain that we're making an offering for a friend of the Western Buddhist Order, so that other people know that we are allowed to just ............ what we do in ........

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: I'm not quite sure what you're referring to.

turns.speaker: Sanghamitta
paragraphs: Well, when Ali died, .....

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Ali is the Friend who died in India?

turns.speaker: Sanghamitta
paragraphs: Yes, that's right. And I wanted to ......... I rang up and said I wanted to make an offering ........... stop them doing that, but I wanted the people of Brighton to know why we are doing it -

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Why it was being done in Archway, you mean?

turns.speaker: Sanghamitta
paragraphs: No, ......... at Archway, but also we could think about Ali in Brighton. And all then is giving more power of thought and sending out metta to Ali, ...........................lost contact and didn't agree with it, and I think they didn't also appreciate that I had got quite a deep affection for Ali, and I would have liked to have done this, and I think there should be room for spontaneous gestures of this kind ......... People come into the Friends know what we do .....

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: I'm not quite sure what you're trying to say or to ask.

turns.speaker: Sanghamitta
paragraphs: Well, you know, the people who come to the Brighton meetings, nobody's ever died there, and nothing has therefore been done. If I had just explained that we were making an offering for a Friend of mine who was a Friend of the Western Buddhist Order.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well, I don't see why you shouldn't have been able to do so, or ......... if you wanted to.

turns.speaker: Sanghamitta
paragraphs: Yes, but their powers of thought going as well.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well, why didn't you arrange it in Brighton, then?

turns.speaker: Sanghamitta
paragraphs: Well, I wanted to, but I was - you know - stopped from doing it.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: But who stopped you?

turns.speaker: Sanghamitta
paragraphs: Well, Buddhadasa got on the phone, and apparently ...... London, and they said no.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: But London's got nothing to do with it.

turns.speaker: Sanghamitta
paragraphs: Archway said it was enough we were doing a memorial service.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: OK, well you just find out the time and you do what you want to do at the same time. Meditate.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: There's nothing to stop you doing it on your own.

turns.speaker: Buddhadasa
paragraphs: ....................... I told her it was something that we couldn't introduce at the normal regular business, because this would set a precedent. Nobody invited you - Ali ? Nobody ........... [I said] I'll ring up London and see what they think. I talked to Subhuti on the phone, he said it's not really that important that you do it in Brighton, because we're doing one up here in London, a proper one, to celebrate ..... If we have somebody popping off in Brighton we'll give them the full treatment. But until that happens - you know - But there's nothing to stop you making a personal offering to the shrine.

turns.speaker: Sanghamitta
paragraphs: Yes, I did that, but ..... I also said something to let the people know that their power of thought could go out -

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes, but I don't think that would be very meaningful when they didn't know her.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: And also you might encourage a sort of indulgence there, which .......

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: ........ people in hospitals in Brighton are dying, or -

turns.speaker: Sanghamitta
paragraphs: Well, no, it was a Friend of the Western Buddhist Order.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: No, it was not as though it was a friend of somebody - I didn't even know her myself, I'd never met her. But those who knew her in London I know very much wanted to do something, so therefore they did it. But I don't know whether it would have been very meaningful to ask people in Brighton who'd never heard of her before to suddenly do that. In your case, it's fine that you did it, because you knew her and you felt something, but I think that's quite enough.

turns.speaker: Sanghamitta
paragraphs: Well, I was going to, as I say, in a very short time explain that she had gone out to India and -

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: I don't see the point of it, for people who didn't know her.

turns.speaker: Sanghamitta
paragraphs: I was going to get their thoughts going -

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: But they didn't know her, so I don't think there would have been much point in it.

turns.speaker: Sanghamitta
paragraphs: Do we have to know anybody - it's a difficult field(?), I .....

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well, if you feel you can do something, even without knowing the person, fine, but I think the majority of people, being asked to direct their thoughts to someone they'd never seen and never heard of before, they wouldn't have known where they were, sort of thing; they'd have
felt it all rather unreal.

turns.speaker: Sanghamitta
paragraphs: Yes, I suppose I was wanting them to know that we have a way of recognising this in the Friends of the Western Buddhist Order.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: It's good that they should know that, but I think the time and place to explain it has to be chosen quite carefully.

turns.speaker: Lokamitra
paragraphs: It will come sooner or later.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes, it will come sooner or later. I certainly feel that we should do whatever is needed for everybody who dies, but I think - I'm not even sure whether this has been held now already in - (Voices.)

turns.speaker: Sanghamitta
paragraphs: I didn't know about that ....... and I felt that something might be done about people who are interested, what it is that we think of ....

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: I think if it isn't someone who was known to the people in Brighton there's not really much point in asking them to participate.

turns.speaker: Sanghamitta
paragraphs: Right, thank you.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Can we come back to Bodhidharma? I'm afraid we're not even half way through our subjects.
: (murmuring)............ we could leave ...... for Saturday, ...........

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Let's see how we get on. We can only do a bit at a time. All right, Bodhidharma Day; have we celebrated it, or did we celebrate it? (Voices.)

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: Vessantara gave a short talk.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: On Bodhidharma?

turns.speaker: Lokamitra
paragraphs: About 20 minutes long. (Voices.)

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Was that a public celebration?

turns.speaker: Lokamitra
paragraphs: It was on a Tuesday night, it wasn't advertised specially; it was our regular main night of the week.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: He just gave a short talk and we did a Puja, and that was it. .....

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well, Bodhidharma's association is definitely with the Ch'an or Zen tradition, so I think if we celebrate Bodhidharma Day, presumably it is with the intention of drawing some attention to that particular tradition and its importance historically, and presumably to some extent for us also. Was anything like that done, does anyone remember?

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: He did in his talk ........ about Bodhidharma ... Chinese ......... It wasn't so much a celebration ..........

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Do you think people have as strong a feeling for Bodhidharma as, say, many of them have for Padmasambhava - or perhaps Hotei, even?

turns.speaker: Voices
paragraphs: No.

turns.speaker: Dharmapala
paragraphs: ..... more in karate circles and things like that there'd be more feeling for him ............. (Voices.)

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: He's one of the leading figures of Buddhism, I think he ought to become somebody we have strong feelings for.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: There are lots of leading figures in Buddhism, though. Hundreds, say.

turns.speaker: Ratnapani
paragraphs: He is particularly significant to us, inasmuch as there seem to be parallels between what happened in China and what is happening here.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: He kept China and Japan quiet - tremendous ....

turns.speaker: Lokamitra
paragraphs: Yes, but so did lots of other people.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: Who?

turns.speaker: Lokamitra
paragraphs: Well, there was - er - (laughter) ........

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well, no, the general principle is: how do we select leading figures from Buddhist history whom we want to celebrate? Well, we could celebrate Ashoka; what about Yuan Chuan? What about Nagarjuna? What about Milarepa? What about Dogen? You see - we'd like to celebrate them all, but it is rather difficult.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: ........ doesn't concern us very much .......... we can't practise ...........

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: Certainly doesn't have the impact that Padmasambhava does.

turns.speaker: Asvajit
paragraphs: I think, on the other hand, it would be a very good thing if somehow or other we incorporated into our Movement recognition of the East. We've got recognition of as it were the West.

turns.speaker: Lokamitra
paragraphs: This will come up in our three main festivals, surely. There will be references to the East all the time: the Buddha, his life, his teaching; the Sangha.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: .... Padmasambhava.

turns.speaker: Asvajit
paragraphs: Yes. I associate this all very much with the West, strange to say. Whereas Bodhidharma I think of as particularly Eastern.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well, Bodhidharma was an Indian.

turns.speaker: Asvajit
paragraphs: Yes, but then he went eastwards.

turns.speaker: Uttara
paragraphs: ....... like if we're celebrating the three main festivals, and we're celebrating Padmasambhava, so I think that gives the Friends a flavour of the Tibetan, but if you celebrate Bodhidharma then you're bringing up also ...............

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes, I think this is also important. Though I would personally say that if we celebrated any figure from the Far East, from China, I personally think it should be Chih-i, the T'ien-t'ai patriarch, who is of much more general importance and significance, and whose tradition is much closer to everything that we're trying to do. I think that the main idea seems to be that

we celebrate the festivals of those figures who are not only very important in their own right in the East, but also stand for the major traditions of Buddhism. In the case of Padmasambhava, it's not only Tibetan Buddhism, it's definitely the whole Tantric component. In the case of Chi-i, it's very definitely Chinese Mahayana, and he is of the greatest importance for the whole of Far Eastern Buddhism, including even Ch'an or Zen, because a great deal of Ch'an teaching and instruction was given at T'ien-t'ai monasteries.
But the whole thing requires very careful study. There are some very heroic Buddhists, very historical characters like Yuan Chuan, whose lives are very inspiring. There's Atisha also.

turns.speaker: Ratnapani
paragraphs: Bodhidharma seems to have been a bit of a myth, as far as one can make out.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: According to some scholars, he is a myth. We know hardly anything about him; we've got hardly any detail.

turns.speaker: Ratnapani
paragraphs: There's no material for celebration ..............

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well, Padmasambhava seems to have assumed a sort of archetypal significance in a way that Bodhidharma apparently hasn't.

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: As it is, though, we've got - the festivals we do celebrate cover the Four Refuges.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: That's true, Padmasambhava is the guru.

turns.speaker: Lokamitra
paragraphs: But I wonder what relevance there is to the Friends, certainly, for celebrating other teachers. Certainly we try and keep Padmasambhava to the Order. And maybe we should celebrate great figures within the Order, but personally I don't feel myself drawn to do that yet, until I've got much more understanding of their place in Buddhism. But at general festivals, I just don't see the point at all. Let's establish the main festivals, and get people celebrating those first.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: The main thing is ..... Mitras. (?)

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Week? (?) (Laughter.)

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: I'd like to ........ that we put more into less.

turns.speaker: Ratnapani
paragraphs: In the meantime, though, it seems as though your choice of Chih-i is something could be - I don't know, it's Devaraja's department - if ....... could be sorted out so that we have got something to work on when we do wish to expand and do wish to include further implications for ......

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Perhaps a bit of study has to precede all this. I have located recently a Life of Ch'i-I published in some oriental magazine quite long ago, and I'm trying to get hold of a copy of this. But he is a historical personage. But perhaps you'd better leave that now. We haven't said anything about those additional days.

turns.speaker: Lokamitra
paragraphs: Just before we get on to those, Bhante, it was suggested that we celebrate the Buddha's leaving home. Nothing was decided on that.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Ah. We have to try to find out on which particular full-moon day that was.

turns.speaker: Lokamitra
paragraphs: And then we should do it perhaps on the scale of the Parinirvana and the Buddha's birthday - like, say, a lecture or something like this which is relevant or -

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Anyway, I think Devaraja's idea in selecting these other additional dates is, inasmuch as the four - Tara, Manjusri, Avalokitesvara, Vajrapani, are the four great Bodhisattvas, and many people are doing their particular practice. But I think this should be left as a more private sort of thing.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: I'd like to still have those envisaged .......... Lokamitra: Can we get - I understood that these dates are taken from the nearest equivalent in the Chinese calendar, and if we're going to have them let's have them from the Tibetan calendar, surely? .......

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well, we could have quite a number of anniversaries just for Order members' individual information, in addition to those that are going to be publicly celebrated, or celebrated within the Order. There's no reason why we shouldn't have as much information as possible.

turns.speaker: Lokamitra
paragraphs: One other short one on festivals, Bhante: just something you said at the last Convention - we should all of us encourage each other to take days off for them, days off work. Sometimes this really isn't possible, but it's not happening very much - at the moment. (laughter.)

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: A few months off!

turns.speaker: Lokamitra
paragraphs: Well at festivals; it doesn't happen on even the main festivals, Wesak Day, Dharmachakra Day and Sangha Day.

turns.speaker: Dharmapala
paragraphs: This was quite covered when we were talking about the legal situation, in that it does seem that it's a matter for the individual and his employer.

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: ........... point Lokamitra's making here is that individuals ought to go to their employer and ask for the day off. It's not a matter of legality or how you arrange it, but simply one should go on and do it.

turns.speaker: Manjuvajra
paragraphs: I don't think you should adopt that attitude. Surely it's up to the individual Order members to choose whether they ........ should do -

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: A reminder is in order, .........

turns.speaker: Vajradaka
paragraphs: Right, because people just may not think of it themselves.

turns.speaker: Vajradaka
paragraphs: But at least you have thought it out.

turns.speaker: Ananda
paragraphs: You've also got to give consideration if you're in this sort of situation in a job, where other people might be inconvenienced. You've got to give that ..... You can't just say 'I'm a Buddhist, I've got to celebrate my festival.'

turns.speaker: Marichi
paragraphs: ..............that particular time you can make preparation for it, and make that part of the preparation, .........

turns.speaker: Lokamitra
paragraphs: I would encourage everyone to take off Wesak! It's the most important day in our calendar, it's a time when all of us should come together wherever we are. You know when it is - at the beginning of the year you have three or four months' notice, so you can save

any trouble at work or anything then, and you miss a day's pay, maybe; but I'd encourage every Buddhist, every member of the Order, to take the day off and celebrate it with other Order members, and I think it's a pretty bad show that lots of people don't!

turns.speaker: Asvajit
paragraphs: Hear, hear.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Hear, hear. (Talk, laughter.) All right, can we now get back if necessary - if anyone has anything further to say about ceremonies? This is a quite important aspect of the Order's functions.

turns.speaker: Vajradaka
paragraphs: I think that someone should say that they are going to put together an Order Puja book, certainly just ..... chants, with the cremation ceremony, the memorial cremation ceremony, the blessing, the ........ , and things like that.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: A few select readings, so that one doesn't always have to go searching through books.

turns.speaker: Lokamitra
paragraphs: Could we have, like the Register, a loose-leaf book so that any new information could be added, any relevant stuff could be added quite easily? And then it doesn't all have to be done at one time; when something comes up or when someone does something, it can then be distributed -

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: This is quite an important point, but rather than just one person take responsibility for it then discuss it with others .....

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: I think it will have to be done by two people; I think the best way might be for Devamitra and me to do it together. What do you say? Because I think I have to give my attention to this, but at the same time I think I'll need quite a bit of help, gathering the material and getting it typed and all that; so I think it might be best if we produced something.
Any further points anyone would like to make about the actual performance of ceremonies, any difficulty encountered, any comments?

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: There's a thought - I should like to see the five lay precepts introduced at Mitra ceremonies. We don't really seem to have anything official where they are introduced. You go straight from just reciting them at the Puja to the Ten Precepts.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: The great doubt in my mind is about the Refuges, that is to say having the Refuges recited by those who don't actually commit themselves. And usually Precepts go with Refuges.

turns.speaker: Ananda
paragraphs: Is that fundamental? Could one not take it upon oneself as a preparation for Taking Refuge?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: One could do that, yes. It isn't usual in the Buddhist world, simply because everybody is Buddhist, so everyone recites the Refuges. But certainly in many passages in the scriptures precepts are mentioned as being propagated by pious kings, without any reference to the Refuges. So it might well be that we could introduce the Precepts without the Refuges, as a sort of general preparation. But they'd have to be - one of the things I feel very strongly about is that whatever we take upon ourselves within the Western Buddhist Order, we must really observe it. We don't want to get into a situation which they have got into in the East where they've got lots and lots of rules and precepts which nobody ever observes, or very few people ever observe. So, for instance, the Five Precepts include abstention from intoxicants: are you going to ask all Mitras to give up intoxicants? Otherwise there's no point in asking them to take that precept. And then, if you are going to ask your Mitras to give up intoxicants, what about you, as an upasaka, because that is not part of the Ten Precepts and you are not required as an upasaka or upasika to be a total abstainer; so are you going to ask your Mitras
to be total abstainers? So what do we do about the Five Precepts?

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: Does it mean total abstention? ......... Because it says .......... awareness ............

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
