turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: I think it would amount to that in the end, because the Mitras at present seem to have a sort of corporate identity, and I think in a quite healthy way, even though we didn't think about that at the beginning; I think it would happen in this case too, though again I don't personally feel unhappy about that.

turns.speaker: Marichi
paragraphs: .............. but I don't see at the moment that that would provide increased facilities beyond that sort of thing for Mitras ................

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: No, I don't think that - Hm, right. Well, the fact is that it would be an even more outer circle, if you like a dotted line. So, yes, inasmuch as the Mitras were a more inner circle, there are special facilities for those who become Mitras. But there wouldn't be special facilities for those who are as it were recognised Friends; they'd just do something presumably, go through some little ceremony which gave them the feeling of being regular Friends, and there it would end.

turns.speaker: Siddhiratna
paragraphs: ......... divided but seen to be different from; what's after the dotted line?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well, for instance, as Marichi said, those who maybe live out in the country somewhere and get the Newsletter, and are certainly in contact with us but not in that way; or maybe come once or twice a year.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: Or even come every other week, .....

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: Very much on a Wednesday night, which is the beginners' night, people come along - it varies considerably from individual to individual, but some people come along, they just do the meditation, maybe in some cases for a month, maybe in some cases only for a couple of weeks. Then they start to come up to you and they want to know more about how to apply in their whole life, how to get into the Friends more -

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: That's presumably where the Mitras come in.

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: No, I ... the point at which they became really -

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: I think we must - (Voices.) No, what has happened is we've upgraded the Mitras also, so when you upgrade something there's still the original level from which you've upgraded, on which there are still some people.
(end of side)
Tape 8, side 2 __________: You think something new has to be created to .....

turns.speaker: Lokamitra
paragraphs: It's only a part of an upward-moving thing, but for those people who really appreciate what we're doing, maybe who live outside London, receive the Newsletter, donate regularly, and really consider themselves supporters of the Friends, and very close to Friends but don't in fact go any further than that?

turns.speaker: Ananda
paragraphs: ...... I think that's quite sufficient. If people are encouraged to work in that way within the body of .... (Voices.)

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: I wouldn't like to refuse to send the Newsletter..... I notice the feeling very much like this when we tell them, 'You are the Friends' - they appreciate it, they are the Friends, ...... they come to the shrine, they are considered to be Friends, they appreciate it very much.

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: I definitely think we should keep that. It's a very strong part of our approach. It does seem to be ..... between that and Mitra.

turns.speaker: Ananda
paragraphs: ...... a corporate body ..... in order to give that .....

turns.speaker: Marichi
paragraphs: ....................supporters, you just have all these people who ........ make an offering .....

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: ..... a feeling in themselves that they are part of it is the main thing.

turns.speaker: Ratnapani
paragraphs: But are they not in fact joining a healthy group at that stage?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes. This is what it would amount to, and quite rightly. Marichi: It does seem a recognition of ...... exists.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: .......... desirable. (Voices.)

turns.speaker: Lokamitra
paragraphs: Can I suggest that we ask Bhante to think about it?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: I'll think about it.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: I rather like the personal offering on Sangha Day. That wasn't to become a Friend - a sort of provisional Mitra. (Voices.)

turns.speaker: Ananda
paragraphs: I think the danger is that we're going to create a whole host, series of steps, names and levels.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: But why do you consider that a danger?

turns.speaker: Ananda
paragraphs: Because I think then you just proliferate a system for its own sake.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: But why do you - you're not doing it for its own sake. We are considering these things because of an actual concrete need, and we are just considering how we can meet the need. We don't start off with an abstract idea of doing things in this way or that way.

turns.speaker: Ananda
paragraphs: I don't feel there's a need for any identifiable corporate body in a sense. What I feel the need for is a sense of involvement and possibly ......, which I think can take place without any additional structure.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: I don't think it's a structure, but what has been suggested is a ceremony, and we know the importance of ceremonies for people at every stage.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: ....... How would that affect those who are outside the ......., ................?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: I don't know, I just haven't considered that.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: ........ and they could just come to London for a day -

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: Or when they come to London, they could - (Voices.)

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: I think if you mention the case of that particular Friend, I'm quite convinced that in the case of a person like that, a ritual acknowledgement of what already exists makes a tremendous impression, and you'll have that person for life. I'm quite sure of that.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: So how would that come about, with ...... ?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Whenever they - well, as far as I can think at the moment without having given it previous thought, there would just have to be the little ceremony or whatever it was when they did happen to come in contact with the Movement.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: ............. very, very careful consideration just how we present it. I think it's critical.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well, I think it requires very careful thought, for that reason. And I may feel the need to think for another year, even. But I will think.

turns.speaker: Padmaraja
paragraphs: Another point. The possibility of some kind of token like a black cord or whatever, some sort of cord for Mitras and kalyana mitra.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: You mean something analogous to the kesa?

turns.speaker: Padmaraja
paragraphs: Yes. Could we think about that? Possibly for Mitras.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: We've already thought about it, .........

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well, maybe, but - No, I don't have any strong idea, but - (Voices.)

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: ........ upgrading the Mitras system to the level of the ordinations where they were a few years ago, and it would seem to be in order to have some sort of physical recognition ........ some sort of token ..........

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: Perhaps we could give them a rosary.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well, not everybody might care to use [it].

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: Is there a question about .......... upasaka .......... ? (Voices.)

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: When you said cord, do you mean a cord like ........?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes, there are some ceremonies of blessing, and of course when you become a Mitra you get a blessing; a thread is tied round the wrist, after the recitation of the Mangala and Metta Suttas, and that when the thread was passed round the circle then a section of it is taken and tied round your wrist - that's the sort of thing that Padmaraja has in mind.

turns.speaker: Marichi
paragraphs: It's like a ring, isn't it, rather than ..... ?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: In a way. I don't know - could one even consider a kesa of a different colour? I'm just
thinking aloud now.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: A cream-coloured one!

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: No. (Voices.) What I felt was - I'm only thinking aloud, so I don't want any sort of, you know, reactions - I was thinking of a thinner, green, kesa, like green is the colour of nature; so you come as it were into the room as a healthy, natural person, without any spirituality but just normal and healthy.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: Haha. (Laughter.)

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: There's no harm in giving them something to live up to. But ....... my thinking aloud.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: Green's the colour of Going for Refuge, actually.

turns.speaker: Sanghamitta

When you consider the difficulty that I've had ............ somebody would want to become that Mitra, .......... for that reason, that they have a thing about somebody of the opposite sex and probably feel that it will give them an opportunity and yet kid themselves that it's a spiritual thing. Also personal content itself, I don't think there's any need for anything more personal in another set of papers...... You get down to the nitty-gritty.

turns.speaker: Lokamitra

But if he hasn't time to write it, we can't help that; it certainly is very useful indeed as it is. (Voices.) I am very dubious about Mitras contributing, because that could very easily become - well, even sometimes Order members' contributions to Shabda leave a lot to be desired; you don't want just to fill up some pages of Mitrata with possibly some misunderstandings, or very immature understandings of the Dharma.

One suggestion that I would like to make, though, for consideration is that there could be a question and answer column, with perhaps Mitras writing in with questions, on the basis of the material, if there's anything that they want explained further or anything that still isn't clear they could write in and say, 'Would you please explain so-and-so?' Or even general Dharma questions that come up and are of interest to a number of people; and either Padmaraja could answer them himself or he could invite an Order member to prepare an
answer or in some cases if there was (break in recording) .......................
(End of session)

Session 5

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: ..... and this morning we come to our third plenary session, which is on the teaching of the Dharma, both theoretical and practical. And there are various subdivisions here. First of all, basic principles, and then the content, structure and leadership of study, as well as meditation, classes, and study courses. So we'll go through each of those in turn.

Let's go first of all into the study classes and things of that sort, the teaching of the Dharma in the more as it were theoretical and almost philosophical sense. This will include, of course, things like lectures.

The first point to be made clear here is that what we teach is basic Buddhism, for want of a better term; in other words, the fundamental principles. It's these that we've got to get clear in people's minds first of all; not so much the teaching of any particular school or any particular sect of Buddhism. I think this point has been made many a time before, but I wonder whether anybody has anything to say about it as the result of their actual experience in the teaching situation, whether in the course of taking a study group or class, or giving a lecture or just answering people's questions about the Dharma.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well, this raises the question of what is the function as it were of the Dharma teaching? And in the case of the teaching as a whole, as it came from the Buddha, one of the points that I have made is that the Dharma was the Buddha's attempt to communicate to others not Enlightened something of his experience of Enlightenment and to communicate it in such a way that they were impelled to move likewise in that direction. So it is this sort of thing that we have to do on our own level and within the limits of our own experience. It is not .... teaching the Dharma as a subject; I think this is a point, really, which is emerging, isn't it? Not teaching the Dharma like you might teach arithmetic or history or geography and so on. Of course, there will be a certain factual content; you'll be having to say that the Buddha lived at such-and-such time and the details of his life were as follows, and that there are such- and-such doctrinal teachings. But through that medium you will be trying to instil a certain overall attitude towards life, a certain feeling.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: I find people don't really have very much interest in the formal aspects of doctrine.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: What do you man by formal?

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: Well, for example, people never ask us to ........ the Noble Eightfold Path or the Four Noble Truths.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Why do you think that is?

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: I think they think it's - there's this idea that Buddhism is more than just doctrine, so they tend to disregard the doctrine.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Ah. Well, this is obviously going to the other extreme, isn't it?

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: Surely that means they have to be encouraged to relate to the doctrine............. (?) That seems to me one of the functions of a study group. It's to make that connection between them, ....... the Noble Eightfold Path, .............. make the bridge.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well, this is the function of the teacher. And this is what I was saying in the course of one of the study seminars - that whoever is leading a study group - and this probably applies to people taking or giving lectures as well - should be able to convert, as it were, the general doctrinal teaching into precepts applicable to the actual spiritual needs of the people to whom you are talking. In other words, in a way you can't give them straight doctrine; in a sense, there's no such thing as straight doctrine. On the one hand, you've got the doctrinal tradition of Buddhism, then you've got your own experience, your own spiritual life, and then you've got your people to whom you are talking. So, with the help of your own experience, you translate the general doctrinal principles into precepts for them to practise.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Oh yes, they must have come alive. If you're just teaching out of the book, well naturally people will think it very dull and very dead. You have to enliven the letter of the teaching with your own experience, otherwise you can't possibly produce precepts for people to apply.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: ................................... (long inaudible speech) And that's much more effective, .............

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: I've been thinking recently, not only do we need to go back to the beginning, but that we need to spend more and more time about less and less. (Agreement.)

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: I think books should be .............

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: When you say books, do you mean scriptures, or books about Buddhism, or do you mean used in the context of the study group?

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: Yes, I mean texts and anything that is written for people to take from books. .......... very little of that, and ............ But be careful not to just go on reading.

turns.speaker: Lokamitra
paragraphs: I think that's too much of a generalisation. If that book is alive for someone, they will be able to convey a great deal from it.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: I think the advantage of a text rightly used is that it does provide a systematic arrangement of material, if it's a good book, and also it provides a point not only from which you can depart but to which you can return.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: There are arguments for not using books, but a minimum ..........

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes. When you say minimum, what do you mean?

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: Well, I think there is a tendency to read, to just go on reading, page after page, and that's what I mean by a minimum, maybe only one page rather than ten, or something like that.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well, obviously a study group is a study group and not a reading-aloud group. Has this been experienced as a difficulty?

turns.speaker: Aryamitra
paragraphs: Mm. The reverse probably.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: What is the reverse? Waffling on?

turns.speaker: Aryamitra
paragraphs: Yes, going on, not .......... back to the - or maybe only covering half a page or something like that, and .........

turns.speaker: Ananda
paragraphs: I don't really think it's anything to do with the quantity of text that you get through, it's the way one experiences ...... (Voices.)

turns.speaker: Ananda
paragraphs: But this is a thing in which I tend to disagree with most people, because I feel it's justified to go off at some tangents, because some people need to relate their own experience, however mundane it might be, to the Dharma; you can't hold them too rigidly to the text.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well it is just a question of the overall mindfulness and skill and tact of the person leading the study, the study group. But it is true that sometimes people need to go off at a tangent, but you must see that they are going off at a tangent and quite consciously allow that to happen; then see when they've gone off at a tangent sufficiently, and then tactfully bring back the discussion to the text. I think sometimes one does have to do this. And what may seem to be tangential in that situation, for that particular person may be very central and very important; so the skilled study group leader will be able to spot that, and make allowances for it. It is quite distinct from allowing someone just to waffle on irrelevantly; it is giving scope to that particular person to express or bring out something which is of considerable importance to him or her, even though not strictly related to what you have been studying. But at least the study has sparked it off. So I think one is quite justified in giving that person scope and even discussing what he or she brings up or introduces. But it's up to the leader to bear in mind what is happening, see what is happening, and when the right time comes, to bring everybody back to the text in a tactful manner, without anybody feeling that they haven't had their say or they've been cut off.

turns.speaker: Ratnapani
paragraphs: The process I've observed is that a tangent will go so far and then begin to die down and the atmosphere becomes sluggish. There then seems to be a choice whether it's led back to the focus, or whether a new tangent from that, a sort of spur from that, is artificially invented to just bring the energy up again, and then you just keep going on.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: So much depends on the skill of the study group leader.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: .......... the actual choice of texts ..................

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Has anyone anything to say about this, their experience with particular texts? - whether they found some that were selected very unsuitable or difficult or vice versa - whether there are any suggestions on these points?

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: I know that ........ Tuesday nights ...............Jewel Ornament didn't work very well.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Why do you think the Jewel Ornament didn't work? [Pause]

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: It's very ...... it's very condensed, ..............

turns.speaker: Siddhiratna
paragraphs: I think Sagaramati said the same thing about the Dhammapada, that that was too condensed, too -

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: The Dhammapada?

turns.speaker: Siddhiratna
paragraphs: Yes, that it was too condensed, too short.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: If it's very condensed, it means that the leader of the group has to do quite a lot of expansion. And if he isn't up to it for any reason, if he either doesn't have the knowledge or is too tired that evening, hasn't sufficient vitality, then of course the whole thing falls very flat. You just pass on to the next verse.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: I think one of the difficulties of the Jewel Ornament is that it's got such a lot of emphasis on having faith in spiritual friends and having faith in almost ecclesiastical ..... And I think people react strongly to that, and so they can't see all the gems in the book. On the other hand, I think the chapters that don't have that in it so much bring out an awful lot.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: ...... a lot of different things .........

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: But I think it's important that when people are having a reaction against the material, you should stop and discuss the reaction. And I think this will enable them then to relate more positively to the material. For instance, if - this instance has been mentioned - if there is this emphasis, or as some people like to say, over-emphasis on faith in spiritual friends, stop and say, 'Why do we all feel it's so difficult to have this sort of faith?' Stop and talk about that for a while, and 'Why do you think it was apparently easy for Indians and Tibetans to have it?' Say 'Are we different from them? Is it due to our cultural conditioning? Are we justified in having less faith? Is all that faith necessary for us?' Sort of ventilate the whole subject quite frankly. You may end up disagreeing with the text. You don't have to agree with every word of a given text.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: Talking about the group, experiences within the group.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: What do you mean by that?

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: Well, that if people are experiencing difficulty ........ difficulty in studying the text, -
(end of side)

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: Talking about the group, experiences within the group.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: What do you mean by that?

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: Well, that if people are experiencing difficulty ........ difficulty in studying the text, -
(end of side)
Tape 9, side 1
- studying the text, but these are just as it were, these are discussed within our group.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: But that's what I'm saying.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: Not much of that has actually happened.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: It hasn't?

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: I don't think so.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: It may be that there haven't been many stumbling blocks of this kind. It's only necessary to discuss in this way if something of this sort does come up. But the possibility should be there, if there is a sort of stumbling block.

turns.speaker: Manjuvajra
paragraphs: I think one of the ..... happened is if somebody - if you're studying something and you find some fundamental character of that book that you can't .... -

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Of the book as a whole?

turns.speaker: Manjuvajra
paragraphs: As a whole, yes; there's a tendency to reject the whole book. S: But if you're uncomfortable with the book as a whole, you shouldn't be studying that text.

turns.speaker: Manjuvajra
paragraphs: Well, actually, I'm really talking from my own experience of the Jewel Ornament of Liberation. I've been reading that on and off for a long time. And I found a strong reaction to it when I first looked, because of this particular point. But after a while I gradually got into it, and I now accept that point. But I find that when I introduce that book to other people, they go through the same sort of thing.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well, perhaps one should then be careful about recommending that particular book. But, as regards study groups, one obviously shouldn't be taking a study group on a text about which you feel basically unhappy, or with which you are in fundamental disagreement on important points, but just leave it aside, take another text. But if they're just incidental points that the group as a whole is not happy with, or even some people are not happy with, just stop and discuss it. For instance, you might get a description of the workings of karma, and say, 'How do we take this? Do we believe this? Do we accept it? Do we take it on faith, or what is our attitude? Or do we just pass it over? How seriously do we take this?' But it's up to the leader of the group to make it clear that this is a part of traditional Buddhist teaching, so therefore let's at least understand it. But we don't expect people to accept it if they're not really able to do that. But at least discuss it, and possibly discuss the reasons why we find it difficult to accept or to understand.

turns.speaker: Nagabodhi
paragraphs: I think this difficulty often arises in the minds of people who are still conditioned by the school attitude of studying a book: that the job is to understand it, not to actually question it if necessary and see how one can relate to it personally. At school you just have to understand ...., valid place in the world ...........

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: Quite a few of those who I've had .......... experience difficulty in studying but said, 'Well, I never liked study when I was at school anyway'. So that does seem to be true.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: When you say study, you mean in the study group?

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: Mm. But it's like they're not able to see that this is a completely different kind of study.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Is this a serious difficulty for a number of people? (Voices agreeing.)

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: .... I would say that what we are doing is understanding, in a real sense, involving one's experience and intuition and feelings as well, but they have no experience of that, coming from school. They have experienced only as intellectual understanding, which is what we call understanding, And that -

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Is study the right word - or do you think we should try to find another?

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: Yes, I've wondered about that -

turns.speaker: Vajradaka
paragraphs: I very rarely use that word study. I always use the word discovery.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: It might well be worth finding other words.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: Except on the programme, we call it that, but in the actual group itself we never call it study.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: How do people feel about the word seminar?

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: That's still got a very academic sound. (Voices.)

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: There was quite a discussion about this three or four years ago, and most people weren't very happy with it, but we couldn't find any other.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: We certainly have no resistance to the word study in our group.

turns.speaker: Lokamitra
paragraphs: ........we always have 'Dharma study'. (Voices.)

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: There may be people who actually like study, and use the word ............

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: I think part of the problem is we haven't always avoided that particular danger. Sometimes it has been study, in a more conventional way. ......... (Voices.)

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: Exploration.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: Yes, it's not really discovery.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: Really, if people are wanting to find out then it doesn't matter what you call it. It's the difference between being forced to do something you're not actually interested in and actually having the problem there of - .......... your meditation, ................. whatever it may be, you are trying to work with in your own ........, and here are words of other people who've ............. on that, and been through that and ............ interested in cars and .... just about to buy a car, .....................

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes. And what we have to be careful about is that people who are really interested aren't given a bone instead of a fish. And some people can come along with real interest and think 'Oh, Dharma study, it must be something really wonderful' - and then they're taken through a rather dry recital of developments of sects and schools, possibly something like that. That's the sort of thing that we have to watch.

So, first of all, careful selection of the text, and then the leader of the group himself or herself being really alive and able to convert that into precepts, to see where the different people in the group are at, to allow time for flying off at a tangent if necessary, and also ability to bring back the discussion to the text, and to use the whole situation as a means to help people clarify their own experience and understanding and to grow and develop. The study group, after all, or Dharma study group, is an instrument of growth, just like meditation. This is what needs to be borne in mind.

turns.speaker: Sanghamitta
paragraphs: I was just talking to ......... before ..... about the way people teach, and I said to you I can't remember all the steps of the Eightfold Path....., and you had to give a way of

doing this which meant something, and ............ more interest to hold some people's minds. And I was saying it took away ......... .... blocks your mind against anything that's called study. Because it wasn't made interesting ......... ...... question, and different teachers can arouse interest by the way they put it across and the way they give it they are able to let you hold it, retain it.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: And of course the teacher has got to be interested himself, this is the first thing.

turns.speaker: Sanghamitta
paragraphs: Yes. But you are able to help people sometimes to retain it, whereas the difficulty is being able to retain something.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: It's more of a situation where you draw something out of people rather than put something into them. .........

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: The teacher, or leader of the study group, is also giving, but is encouraging the other members of the group also to make their contribution. Dharmapala: ............ to education is drawing out.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: .......... education.

turns.speaker: Manjuvajra
paragraphs: What about 'Dharma research'?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: What do you mean?

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: Well, that gives an impression that you're ..... Dharma ......

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Oh, sorry, I thought you meant something quite different - whether we had any ideas about individual Order members doing prolonged research into Buddhism.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: No, I was thinking in terms of different terms, instead of using the word study.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: I don't think ............ (Voices. Chat.)

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: ....................... recorded by several .... instead of just by one. ..........

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: I think perhaps the important thing is to make clear at the beginning of the course what you mean by study, if you're taking a course. Say, we've called it a study group but this is what it actually is; and then really tell them. And live up to that.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Have Order members been finding that the study seminars that I take are helpful in this sense, and that you can then take a study group on the text that is studied with me? Is there a link-up in this way? Or has anyone any suggestion in this respect?

turns.speaker: Ananda
paragraphs: I certainly wouldn't feel that I could take a study group on the strength of attending one seminar, .........rediscovery of the text subsequent to that, going into it over quite a long period, and ..... ...... group with you leading ...........

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: And, of course, eventually there will be the complete record of the thing available.

turns.speaker: Ananda
paragraphs: I think those seminars .......... a lot of ........... material.

turns.speaker: Aryamitra
paragraphs: It's also a discovery for the leader as well. So I don't expect to go into a study
group knowing everything -

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: It would be quite tragic if you did.

turns.speaker: Aryamitra
paragraphs: Yes! I mean you're one as well, you're discovering and so -

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: This is why it's interesting for you as a leader. If you're merely having to repeat again something that you have already worked out and are thoroughly familiar with, it becomes almost a chore, because you also want to discover.

turns.speaker: Lokamitra
paragraphs: Maybe some of the seminar texts are not so appropriate. But some certainly are, and it seems to me that maybe not so much for beginners but certainly others are - seminars present these texts in the way you see them relating to Western .......... at the moment. In a way, it seems to be one of the most important things we can do to carry this over to other people.

turns.speaker: Ananda
paragraphs: I think maybe one wouldn't have to know all about it but one has to have a certain experience of what the text is about.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: It certainly makes sense

turns.speaker: Vajradaka
paragraphs: How did you find your 'Some Sayings of the Buddha' study group ...... ?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Where was this?

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: At Archway on a Tuesday night. And we danced(?) around quite a lot, .......... book.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: You didn't find it too piecemeal?

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: No, not at all. And each passage tended to be the right length, ................

turns.speaker: Nagabodhi
paragraphs: I don't entirely agree with that. I feel it was a bit piecemeal, and also some - I think what Manjuvajra said, I think some people at quite an early stage, came up against things which perhaps they found quite difficult to accept. I think of study text groups that I've been in, that was quite a good one, but it was much better when in fact we dropped text altogether in the next session. I wasn't altogether happy, I felt I personally found it not terribly alive.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: .................... study group with a text as well as without, ............

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: What have you been doing?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Could people speak a bit more about this, because this is a comparatively recent development, and not everybody may be familiar with it? So perhaps something could be said about that.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: ..................... It was up to the group leader to decide what .......... aspects of the Dharma .................... ..... a short talk ............... .................. half a page of notes ......... and maybe come back again to ........... Sometimes the discussion would actually lead back to the main text. ........................

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: So do you think that this might replace the study text, or go along side by side with it?

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: ..... side by side. I think it worked for that - people ..... that ... people were, ................ study text. .....................

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: I think also there is the point that perhaps people, especially newcomers, require a more direct contact with whoever is leading the group, and with his thoughts or her thoughts or experience.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: That seems to be what .................

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Has this been tried anywhere else, apart from Archway?

turns.speaker: Vajradaka
paragraphs: Yes. We did that first before we started the study group, because I felt that people did need to have more of a personal contact, so for the first nine months we just went through all the basic formulations - the five spiritual faculties, Eightfold Path, Four Noble Truths, things like that, the Buddha, the Dharma, the Sangha. And I'd give a little talk and then we would discuss it. And after about nine months then we started studying 'Some Sayings of the Buddha'.

turns.speaker: Nagabodhi
paragraphs: That would seem to be the order to do things rather than the other way round ...........

turns.speaker: Uttara
paragraphs: .............the aim of a study group is?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: The aim of a study group? Well, I did touch on this when I said that the study group basically was an instrument of development, a means of development, for all those participating, both for the leader at his particular level and all the others at their level. And this should be the overall orientation. And, in more particular terms, you're using the study group situation as means of development to try to understand the text, what the text is all about, and relating that to your experience and your own growth and development.

turns.speaker: Uttara
paragraphs: ......... getting too much input. Each week you go and study a thing and you come up with ideas .... ....... understood it intuitively ....... put it into actual practice ........... ....... so many precepts ........... so much information, so much ideas, so much practice ..........

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well, this relates to what we were saying earlier on about going more and more thoroughly into less and less. That should be perhaps the tendency.

turns.speaker: Nagabodhi
paragraphs: I certainly feel that our textless study group really was quite a powerful instrument for change, that group leaders found it very stimulating.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: So in a way, in a study text, you're revivifying .........., revivifying the original inspiration put down in the text.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Right, yes. Because after all, the text is not just words. The text represents a slice of somebody's experience, and you've got to be in contact with the experience behind the words if you are the leader of the group, make the words convey that experience, or at least something of that experience, to those in the group. Maybe they are not familiar with the words, not familiar with the idiom, not familiar with the idea. You have to expand, translate, improvise - on the basis of that text, so as to communicate to those studying the text the real meaning of the text, and the experience from which the text ultimately issued. That is the

study group leader's job. And then, of course, to relate that to where people are, and to suggest ways in which they could practise or how it could help them, and so on. There's a lot of improvisation in taking a study group.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: Can you expand on that?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Amplify that. No, I think not; partly because I've got a tooth which is hurting me and it's painful to talk, so I want to talk as little as possible. But maybe somebody else could.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: (laughs) Or put it this way, you can't stick to tramlines when you're exploring mountains. [Laughter] The tramlines may take you up to the base of the mountain, but after that the leader of the party has to find a way ..... Mangala: It means being creative, then, and inspired.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: I think I've a fair idea what it was I wanted to hear, but I just -

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: - wanted to hear it! So far we've talked about study groups, but the Dharma is also communicated or taught through lectures, and there have been quite a few lectures lately. So could we perhaps talk about that?

turns.speaker: Ananda
paragraphs: One thing I've found about lectures is that one needs a great deal of time not just to prepare it but to give it.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: To give it?

turns.speaker: Ananda
paragraphs: Yes. It's a ..... whole area. Personally, I need ........... researching in various areas quite broadly. And then perhaps the actual preparation of the lecture in terms of writing it only takes a day or two.

turns.speaker: Vajradaka
paragraphs: One suggestion that Subhuti gave to me which I found invaluable - he said that at one time he said 'I try and write as little as possible before the lecture.' And before he said that, I'd been writing for three months before I gave my lecture; I did a big pile of writing, and so when it actually came to the time to give the lecture I just had too much material. But when I tried this approach of just keeping it, turning it over in my mind and then writing right at the very last moment, it just came out right.

turns.speaker: Abhaya
paragraphs: I found that the organisation of material was extremely difficult. Well, the first lecture I'd given there was a tendency to over- ..... in a way - I had a lot of material ...... then the organising of it into a linear form, expressible formulations, quite difficult. And I think one mistake I made, too, was not to consider the audience to which I'd be giving the lecture; what sort of people it would be composed of, whether beginners, or people who ............ some familiarity with it. I think this is important, to really be aware, right from the start, of what the audience will be, or some idea. And also as I say, the organisation of the material itself I found very difficult.

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: There has been a quite noticeable tendency in a number of lectures that I've heard for it to be over the heads of the majority of people in the audience. (Voices.)

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: In what sense, over the heads? Could you say ........?

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: Well, you assume too much about their understanding of and interest in and commitment to Buddhism.

turns.speaker: Vimalamitra
paragraphs: Isn't there a tendency to start the lecture from quite simple ideas and then work up from that anyway? That gives a solid foundation, quite a simple solid foundation to build up to, and as you get to the end of the lecture you ............

turns.speaker: Ananda
paragraphs: I think the same principle applies to ............... Dharma study group. ........................... one very simple principle, like karma - (loud and prolonged laughter)

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: It was years before I dared to give a lecture on karma. ............... (Voices. Comments.)

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: Could we get together afterwards?

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: Not to try to go off on .................

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: I think, you see, one of the things that is happening is that in the earlier days of the Movement we were more dependent upon Buddhism as it ......... (drop-outs on tape), because there was less experience. But now quite a few people have a certain experience of their own from which they can speak, or they can speak about quite fundamental and simple topics and aspects of the Dharma in the light of their own experience, and they don't need to bring in so much of traditional Buddhist doctrinal presentation. So perhaps that should be more and more the tendency. For instance, if you speak about Going for Refuge, you don't need to quote Shantideva and Nagarjuna, and discuss the development of the Refuges in different Buddhist countries, and so on. You just say what Going for Refuge means, for you, and in this country, in the West.
I have also suggested, as I think most of you know, that each and every upasaka and upasika should have ready three lectures which they can give at a moment's notice.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: It's gone up! (Some laughter.)

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: It's gone up to three.

turns.speaker: Vajradaka
paragraphs: Karma is the first!!

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: First of all, a general talk about Buddhism, perhaps with beginners, such as you might give if you were invited to speak at a school or college and so on. And then a talk about meditation. And thirdly, a talk about some topic of your choice - say, 'Buddhism and Art' or 'Buddhism and Society' or 'The Sunyavada Doctrine' or 'Going for Refuge' or anything of your own choice or special interest. And just have the notes of these by you and be so familiar with the topics that if there's a need at very short notice for a Buddhist talk you can just go and give it, without even having to think about it beforehand.

turns.speaker: Vajradaka
paragraphs: I'd like to say something about the specialised - the third one ....., the specialised talk. Something that I did with my first lecture was that I just rearranged all the information and cut out bits of my original lecture in all my subsequent lectures, so that it had the same title but it was a completely different talk each time. And I found that as a result of that I became far more familiar with the actual essence of what was really meant by the title. And I'm sure I could go on for the next ten years using the same title!

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: How many people present have not yet given a public lecture? It's about half, isn't it, or a bit more than half? Who has? Hm, yes, about a third have and two-thirds haven't. That's not too bad, I suppose. But I think sooner or later everybody, practically, will have to have had this experience. So you'd better get your three talks ready.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: I was wondering - what you said, Subhuti, about a lot of the talks that have been given were over the heads of most people, if in fact these talks were of this third type
that Bhante mentioned, in other words a talk of our own choice rather than being a specific topic.

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: I don't think necessarily so. I think all of these could be given at a number of different levels. I think it's just, like Abhaya said, .... really aware of ........ takes a while to realise.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: I think it's also a question of not only what you present - for instance, the doctrine of sunyata and so on - but your lead up to it. Because I think you can communicate something about that, even to people who know nothing of Buddhism, provided you pave the way carefully enough. But that may take quite a long time.

turns.speaker: Ratnapani
paragraphs: There's a principle I was told in writing up notes at school for experiments, that you should assume the person reading - I think it applies to the person listening - is intelligent but utterly ignorant, and start from there.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: That's a very good point, yes.

turns.speaker: Nagabodhi
paragraphs: This is one of the difficulties and one of the blessings of integrating Buddhism, the Dharma, into our experience - that something that seems quite natural to us might be quite strange and unnatural to somebody who may be very intelligent but who isn't a practising Buddhist. And this is the sort of assumption one should make; it's not just one of intellectual sophistication but a totally different language of looking at the world.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: I think it's a very good discipline in this area, not to make any assumptions at all ......... (Voices.)

turns.speaker: Vajradaka
paragraphs: I think if you are a well-informed person, if you listen to the radio occasionally and read a newspaper, you know basically the common world views, and you can relate your viewpoint to those.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: It's very important, actually, ................. You've got something ..................., quite often.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: I used to find when I was in India among the ex-Untouchables, any reference in the course of a lecture to local happenings or local personalities went down very, very well indeed, because it showed that you were in touch with them and their lives.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: ........ remember what you said ........ ..........

turns.speaker: Padmapani
paragraphs: It seems very important that when a person is giving a first lecture, that other Order members can come along not only as a supportive ......., but also to suss out, so to speak, the atmosphere and what sort of people they are. Sometimes - well, I haven't done a lecture - it seems to me that the person might not be able to fathom that, that side of the .... so much as Order members who are mixing in with one another, and it will help the dharma quite a lot ...... if you get a lot of feedback from actual people who are there, ....... this is what my lecture is all about, what do you think about that? but they were actually there to sort of soak it up.

turns.speaker: Ratnapani
paragraphs: ....that always happens at Archway ...

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: ................. quite a good idea of the type of people that can come along.

turns.speaker: Padmapani
paragraphs: ............ question ........... this factor of ...... you don't know who you're speaking
to. Surely Order members will have - listening and soaking up the atmosphere then they'll have a good understanding ..................

turns.speaker: Devamitra
paragraphs: I think maybe I am not quite sure of feeling not particularly associated with the Archway Centre. When I gave my lecture I knew who I was talking to and what sort of people were going to be there, ...........

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: ............................. ....... actually got there to give a lecture, and you can feel who people are, and you feel who they are and then you have to adapt your lecture to them. .............

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Right. This is why you go along with a skeleton of notes, and you may have to change your approach at a minute's notice when you see a sort of audience quite different from what you'd expected.

turns.speaker: Vajradaka
paragraphs: Just to carry on from that: when I gave that lecture in Aberdeen and in London, before I went to those two places I rang up and wrote and said, What do the people there, the people who are likely to come to the lecture, what do they really need? What kind of people are they? Asking a trusted person - the people who are in with that group. And each time the person I asked was I felt quite right; they gave me something very useful, and I just trusted them and modelled the talk on what they said.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: I used to do this among the ex-Untouchables before I went to any particular place. I'd ask especially what was the local situation, had they any specific problems or troubles, and so on; the particular character .... For instance, in a certain place they might all be the members of a particular sub-caste, and they might have certain leading personalities from that particular area, and you'd have to know all that. Or there might have been certain clashes in the past. If one did this, it put you very much in the picture. And you can also avoid pitfalls and speak in a way that was very relevant and interesting.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Very often they do it anyway. They give you a idea, especially colleges, I've found, that you'll be talking to such-and-such people who know so much about Buddhism; or at least we've done so much study.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: If you actually talk to the person who's asking you, you ........., then they ..... and change that, we've actually done two classes on Buddhism; but what I want ...................... which is quite different from the original ....... then you can go into that situation in much more .... ........

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Sometimes you find that people in that situation are not nearly so interested in learning about the history of Buddhism, which they can do with their own teacher, but what makes you a Buddhist? How did you come into it? How did you discover it? What difference does it make? And sometimes even if you just talk in a very ordinary way about these things - sometimes - that is much more interesting to them than any amount of academic dissertation.

turns.speaker: Abhaya
paragraphs: You think that would be a good choice for the introductory talk?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: What's that?

turns.speaker: Abhaya
paragraphs: 'Why I am a Buddhist'.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: It might be in some situations. But one has to be careful that it's a sort of communication, and it doesn't give the impression of you trying to proselytise them. But as Gotami said, suss
out the situation first, and gather whatever information you can. It may be that that is just what is needed; or it may not be.

turns.speaker: Manjuvajra
paragraphs: I gave a series of talks at a school. I started off with 23, and it was a six-week course, and I finished up with two. ............

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well, why not? What do you think - ?

turns.speaker: Manjuvajra
paragraphs: Well, it's the same thing, ....... much too high, completely outside their experience, and - well, just that.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: I think this raises something very general about our teaching that I've touched upon before: that we seem to be getting more and more away from historical Buddhism as doctrinally formulated, and much more into speaking in terms of our own experience, maybe illuminated by passages from the scriptures and so on.

turns.speaker: Lokamitra
paragraphs: I think school is - I gave a talk ......... they are so interested, certainly ....... I wish I could have given them so much more. It's quite a good feeling, in a way, certainly an experience, because they are, they're alive, ..... They want to know.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: There's also another point that occurs to me, and this is what has been called the step-by- step approach, in communicating the Dharma. How important does one think that this is - that you start systematically at the beginning, and gradually build up? And, of course, if so, what is the beginning or where is the beginning? (Voices.) What have people found in their actual experience?

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: ............. general view of Buddhism, ......... it's about Higher Evolution ........... more specific. ........ Eightfold Path and ............

turns.speaker: Vajradaka
paragraphs: This is probably quite important for a person who is beginning a lecture series to remember, because if the people, like in this last lecture series at Archway, are coming from all over Britain to give a lecture series; in a way they hadn't conferred, there wasn't any common, really common, denominator, except that it was on different subjects. But I think that it probably would be quite helpful for the people who are going to be listening to the lectures, every lecture, to have in the first lecture one which is a bit general.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: What was the first one in the last series?

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: Mine.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yours, and you're one of our ............

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: I think it was quite sort of -

turns.speaker: Nagabodhi
paragraphs: ........ in the previous lecture series, that is the way it went. It started with the theme of Buddhism. .........

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Do you think we are giving enough attention to lectures, public lectures? Nagabodhi: We're giving it all we can, certainly in Archway. (Voices.)

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Do you think we are going out enough?

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: ....... lectures in other places, ....

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: What I feel is that there are probably two sort of great means of drawing people, or attracting people. One is meditation, and I think the other is lectures. And certainly this was my own experience when I was in London, both before the Friends were started and afterwards. Sometimes it seemed almost as though more people were interested in lectures as a starting point than even in meditation. So I sometimes wonder whether we are not neglecting that a bit. I'm not thinking so much in terms that there should be regular lectures at the Archway Centre, but that certain people who are known to be quite good as lecturers should make a point of going out or accepting or even inviting invitations, and having a very forceful and fundamental sort of lecture which told what we are all about, and what we do stand for, in very forthright terms. With appropriate follow-up.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: Certainly we are beginning to move out more than we actually did.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: You yourself have stopped giving lectures .............. actual Order members and Friends, but I thought that you had ............ going out and giving the odd lecture .........................

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes, for me - because there was so much else to do.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: But not in general?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Not in general, no.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: I don't know, I think sometimes one can be a bit random and a bit experimental. Sometimes almost as if .... give a lecture in what seems to be a quite unpromising place, and see what happens. I think there's a great deal to be said sometimes for randomness. Sometimes a lot happens as a result of apparent accident. So I think room should be left for randomness, and you shouldn't always do what seems to be the reasonable thing. Just sometimes, if you like, just for fun, give a lecture in what seems to be a completely unsuitable place, and see what happens. [Laughter] Well, just see. Because sometimes one thinks that something is suitable or unsuitable, just as a result of conditioning.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes, that is true, but then there is the follow-up in the form of the Newsletter, and if people are really interested they can be told about retreats, they can be invited to visit the London Centre or any other Centre, or to start up a Centre in their own area; arrange to invite another Order member to give another lecture. Or say 'Would you like to organise a retreat? I'll come back and take it if you get together six or eight people. I'll come back with some tapes and we'll do some meditation' - and so on. I think also we have to be a bit adventurous in this way.
(end of side, side 2)

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: .......... definitely, people are .............

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: Quite soon you'll have a couple of people wandering round.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: That would be good. (Voices.) I think we want for the general public consumption, as it were, very clear, forthright lectures about what we basically stand for and are trying to do,
with a minimum of reference to traditional Buddhist doctrine.

turns.speaker: Sanghamitta
paragraphs: People have expressed quite recently in Brighton .... that ............. particularly .... lectures rather than on ..... And ...... is a need for rather shorter lectures.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well, I don't suppose I'll be giving them, but maybe others could.

turns.speaker: Sanghamitta
paragraphs: No, possibly some of these people could bear this in mind to fill this need. So short, sharp. ...........

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: You haven't ever thought of doing anything like that yourself?

turns.speaker: Sanghamitta
paragraphs: Well, I have, but I haven't gotten around to it! (laughs)

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: I think it would be really good.

turns.speaker: Sanghamitta
paragraphs: Yes, I know. ........... get it together ........

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well, if you had to talk then you'd get it together.

turns.speaker: Bodhisri
paragraphs: Has anyone tried to give a lecture using ............ drawing charts on the wall and using slides and ....... ?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: I sometimes have drawn charts -

turns.speaker: Sanghamitta
paragraphs: The Wheel of Life, you've done.

turns.speaker: Bodhisri
paragraphs: Yes, that's what I mean, the Wheel of Life, .....much better -

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: I've given lectures on the Wheel of Life using slides, using a series of slides which picked out each part that I was talking about.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: Using the poster?

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: Yes, I took photographs of the poster, blew them up.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: If you're talking about specific points, I've tape-recorded the Prajnaparamita and played that, and then showed slides ........... and gave a brief explanation of what the deities were about. It seemed to go down very well.

turns.speaker: Sanghamitta
paragraphs: .......... the Wheel of Life because it's already got together, ............. .................

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: At some other session we'll be talking about sort of as it were audio-visual means of communication, not just the verbal conceptual. The imaginative also.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: I think that with the amount of thangkas and visual material ...... Buddhists we could very easily do that ........ slide and tape show of, say, the Buddha's leaving home. I think it would go down very well.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: This is verging on the arts, and we're going to have a whole session about that. One point - you referred to the Higher Evolution. I'd just like to inquire to what extent people find it useful in talking to people about the Dharma to speak in terms of what I call the Higher
Evolution, rather than in more traditional terms?

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: It's very useful as a sort of backbone(?) which, ...........

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Anybody got any reservations about it?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: But not if you make clear the different levels of consciousness.

turns.speaker: Ananda
paragraphs: I can't hear you.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: People who - you know - politically-minded people who - .........................

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Didn't this come up in Finland, Bodhisri? Vajrabodhi wrote me a letter about it, that in Finland people are very resistant to the idea of anybody being more developed than anybody else. Perhaps you don't have it quite so much in England, but no doubt there is the odd person who objects to the idea of anybody being more evolved than he is.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes. But Vajrabodhi suggested a way round this. He said that comparisons should not be made between one person and another but between your own present and future stages of development, and that the emphasis should be there in the case of such people.

turns.speaker: Bodhisri
paragraphs: Yes. And also a way round it in the context of evolution, one can make it quite clear to them that although somebody may be at a given stage now, but potentially the others ...... that stage as well, and one can make it clear ......... I think evolution is very useful to get over that point as well.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Also, of course, in the long run, as I have pointed out to Vajrabodhi, we mustn't forget to explode this micchaditthi that everybody is equal. You might let them get away with it for the time being and mitigate what you have to say, but in the long run we have to make it clear that when you feel uncomfortable about the idea of somebody being more developed than you this is highly unskilful, and it precludes the possibility of faith - faith in the sense of reverence and looking up to something which is higher than yourself.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: Some people can accept that, but can't accept that they may be more developed than someone else.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: That they themselves are more developed?

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: Yes.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: False modesty.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: Well, no, that they have changed, and that they have grown.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well, lack of confidence, and - one sees so much of this. You have to tell people, no, you are better than you think. This is what one has to say much of the time: you are getting on much better than you think you are. You are not doing so badly. Don't grovel. Don't denigrate yourself, don't run yourself down. Appreciate yourself; even be proud of yourself.

turns.speaker: Bodhisri
paragraphs: .......... very much .... about explaining to people that they can develop together the power ........... more developed or whatever ....... ............... economic power or whatever.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: But they think perhaps of development in terms of becoming stronger, and sort of using your strength against other people.

turns.speaker: Bodhisri
paragraphs: Yes, that is what they are thinking.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: Maybe this is started off by such phrases as 'the superior man'.

turns.speaker: Bodhisri
paragraphs: Yes, they hate that .........

turns.speaker: Ananda
paragraphs: But you can't tell people, in ..... at least, .......... you can't tell them about the higher state of being. You've got to lead the way in which they can actually sense that higher state of being.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Most of the people who make this sort of objection are sort of intellectuals, for want of a better term, so you have to convince them of the theoretical possibility of a higher stage of development for all, which involves the possibility that others might already have achieved that step, and they might also.

turns.speaker: Siddhiratna
paragraphs: Isn't the idea of equality in mankind contained in the idea of Buddha-nature - that everybody -

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: That's the metaphysical foundation, but then most people don't accept the idea, or don't know about the idea, of Buddha-nature, and if you explain it to people prematurely they might interpret it in terms of a sort of empirical equality, rather than in terms of something very deep and spiritual. I think it's quite dangerous to tell people that they've got the Buddha- nature. They then start thinking of it as a sort of possession, thinking that they've got it just because they've understood the idea abstractly. I think it's much safer to speak in terms of development, and that you are not like that now but you can become that. Speak in terms of growth, not that 'You really are Buddha, you've only got just to see the fact.' I think this doesn't work, I don't think this works this way. But certainly speak in terms of potentiality: that you've got a great potential, you can grow, you can develop. But not that you, in some sense, are already that.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: You think that's to be avoided?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: That's to be avoided. We went into this at one of the seminars. I think maybe this is one of the extracts that we ought to take out and print in Shabda, perhaps very soon. Does anyone remember which seminar that was?

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: ........... just transcribed ...

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: No, it's another one, recently. .... Udana much more thoroughly. (Voices.) There's a quite
lengthy discussion about this - it must have been in the recent Udana seminar. (Voices.) Yes, but that is the lengthiest discussion, in the Udana seminar.

turns.speaker: Mangala
paragraphs: ................... telling people that they really are Enlightened, and they've just got to realise it.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: We sometimes come up against this in its Zen form, because people may come to us after being familiar with Zen literature, having read 'You are Buddha', and sometimes we just have to deal with it for that reason.

turns.speaker: Mangala
paragraphs: Yes, I know, but I thought ...... been through all this ages ago, and ..... realised then that it was a popular micchaditthi which everybody was aware of. ...........

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: I'm not so sure that everybody is clear about it.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: ......... that don't know what's happened in the past, for instance Siddhiratna wasn't aware of it. So all these points, I think, you can go on and on and keep going over them, because ................. It's good for everyone to hear them again ..........

turns.speaker: Bodhisri
paragraphs: I think we should really quite concentrate on elaborating the concept of development, ...... I feel that ......... idea of empirical equality ....... everywhere in the West, and at present in biological and also psychological sciences people are very much working on the concept of development, which is something that - something is there, like an embryo develops, it has only one cell but it has the potentiality of a human being coming from biology. And psychological science gives the same concept. And we really use that concept as well: there is the potentiality of the Buddha, although we are not there, and then there are stages. And I think with that development happening .... Western sciences ..... to get across to people ...............

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: It is certainly a familiar language.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: Yes, and it is developing in various ................. in the Western world, ............

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: .......... that sometimes the idea of such evolution is a linear thing, where you're going from one place to the other; whereas perhaps if you could put it over as more expansion -

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: There is that, too. That is as it were the complementary aspect, of unfolding from a centre. Yes, this is true.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: The linear thing gives rise to all sorts of authority ...........

turns.speaker: Bodhisri
paragraphs: ...... beware that we don't get trapped by a rigid status structure, either, because ............... all the time and ..... and although there are phases ........ comparing ........ ..............

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: I think we have to be rather cautious here, and sometimes not hesitate to oppose wrong thinking, even though very, very widespread. However tactful we are about introducing our own ideas, that in the long run we may have genuinely to disagree with some of the most fundamental assumptions of modern thought, especially as regards egalitarianism.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: What's egalitarianism?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: The belief that everybody is equal. And also make it clear, yes, that we believe that everybody should be equal before the law; that everybody should have equal opportunity - sure; but that is quite different from saying that we believe that everybody is equal, in the sense that all human beings are the same in respect of development.

turns.speaker: Bodhisri
paragraphs: But in the eyes of history there has been some connection with ........ has been highly developed spiritually have also had the power.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: I don't know, have they?

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: They may have been ............

turns.speaker: Bodhisri
paragraphs: But they have sort of been used .........

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: But that is again different from a higher degree of development. You've simply got an ecclesiastical position. That is quite different; a social position, really.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: I think that's another point that has to be made very clear, the difference between an ecclesiastical hierarchy and evolution.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: That's more of where you are on the seesaw, up or down; you're still on the Wheel rather than developing.

turns.speaker: Bodhisri
paragraphs: I used the wrong word, I used the word 'spirit', ......

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Religious.

turns.speaker: Bodhisri
paragraphs: Yes, religious or ..... educate..... (?)

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: We have to make clear the point that social and even ecclesiastical organisation does not necessarily reflect spiritual development.

Just one point I'd like to make before coffee, or it's more like an inquiry than a point. Someone mentioned people with analytical minds, asking questions about Buddhism, especially with some sort of political background. And recently I've been thinking that there are, especially among young people, many who for want of a better expression one could call political idealists; perhaps rather naïve but with tremendous energy, sincerity, enthusiasm. We seem to have practically no contact with such people: why? Or how could we perhaps have contact?

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: We've got a few in Brighton.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: You've got a few? That's good.

turns.speaker: Vajradaka
paragraphs: We've got a few in Glasgow. (Voices.)

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: You know the sort of people I mean?

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: ........ have to apply to ........... ideals ......

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: This is why they're at meditation classes anyway, or they're coming along. ............... But they're very reluctant to give up their ideas, ........... have to do.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: But are we making any attempt actually to contact such people, or at least to be in contact with them and to let them know that we exist, as it were, that we're not just a sort of religious organisation?

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: It has certainly started to happen in Bethnal Green, as we're moving in there. The very people who we've contacted are these types ....

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Ah, that's very good.

turns.speaker: Lokamitra
paragraphs: It's probably, I should think, more out of London than in London, it would be easier.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: In London ........

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: Universities and colleges and things like that.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: I think when you're out of London it's quite simple to cut through all strata of society and go from top to bottom, ...... in all sectors of society - not only ......... contact with ....

turns.speaker: Nagabodhi
paragraphs: I think it's also a matter of when political idealists can actually see us doing something, in terms of providing an alternative, not simply in the shrine room or in our minds but in the world - that's when they can take notice. I know several people like this, and I think that they are half-prepared to accept what I say, that consciousness will change society, society - ..........

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: But changes in society help changes in consciousness, and we say -

turns.speaker: Nagabodhi
paragraphs: But they've got all their eggs in one basket, we only put some of them.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: I think we must make it clear that we are not like some movements that maintain that it all comes from consciousness; all that you've got to do is to change your mind, and then everything will be changed. We don't say that. We also say that in order to help at least the beginner to develop his consciousness, certain more favourable conditions are necessary than we find now; so therefore we would like to change certain things in society, very radically indeed, to make possible or to make more easy and more natural this higher development of consciousness.

turns.speaker: Nagabodhi
paragraphs: But I think almost as soon as we start making some kind of impact in that way - at the moment all we can point to is retreats, for example; we can take some people away on retreats, but as soon as perhaps we have a farm going, some kind of business -

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: And neighbourhoods, Buddhist neighbourhoods.

turns.speaker: Nagabodhi
paragraphs: Right - I think then it's going to attract a lot of interest.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: I think this is very important.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: .......... have to overcome this vague materialism that has - young people ....

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: I was going to say that I met one of the Buddhists from ...... I think you could call ........... in the West recently, who said, 'I've met a lot of these Buddhists, and I think most
of them were associated with the Friends.' He said that none of them seemed to do anything. So this made me reflect -

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: I've had the same problem from ........... social changes and social work.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Because at the same time - we can quite accept that criticism - at the same time there are two things to be said. One is we do not believe in activism, in other words doing things for the sake of doing things. And also, if we do anything, we want it to be really good and really helpful; and I'm quite sure that many of the things that are found in the name of social service, helping people etc., don't really help them at all; they just keep somebody happily busy.

turns.speaker: Bodhisri
paragraphs: But that's in the area of ........ we do something, and if one says that ..... isn't really helpful to them, ......

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well, they feel that you're negating them, ......

turns.speaker: Bodhisri
paragraphs: Yes, they don't want to really consider them because they find ............ not in the real sense, in the legal(?) sense.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: I think the most common one I've heard is ......... ...........................

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: There's also the point that not everybody can do everything. I remember when I was in the train once in India on my way to the ex-Untouchables, I met somebody who was running a leper colony, and he wanted to know why I wasn't running a leper colony. So I made this point to him that I've got my interests and my work, which is among the ex-Untouchables; that these people were completely degraded and now, thanks to Buddhism, they are gradually being elevated in every sense - economically, socially and so on.
